record.description: Robert Lee a Hawaii native recounts growing up in Hawaii, and his experiences during the attack on Pearl Harbor. Lee describes the event as seen from his family's home right across the harbor. Lee shares details about life in Hawaii during the period before the attack, and how life changed after the attack. Robert Lee also gives a brief family history and shares memories about his time working in the pineapple plantations as a child, his travels in California, and other aspects of civilian life in Hawaii before the start of World War II.
record.transcript:
DUNHAM: So today is July 26, 2014, and we're here in the lovely home, outdoors on the lovely property of Robert Lee, who's been gracious enough to invite us today for an oral history interview for our World War II Home Front project. And we usually start at the beginning, so I'd just like ask when and where were you
LEE: I was born in Honolulu, in 1921, on October 1. I'm the third child of a family of four children. My father was Alfred G. Lee, and he was born in Salt Lake City. He was actually a junior. His father, who fought in the Civil War, my grandfather, was a trained civil engineer. After the Civil War was over, he and his three brothers all emigrated to the West, to the area in Wyoming closest, I guess, to Salt Lake City, and also to Evanston, Wyoming. My great-grandfather, my grandmother's father, actually organized and settled Evanston, Wyoming. He had been a pioneer, a true pioneer out there. Had come from New York and from the Michigan areas, with a supply of oxen and so forth, intending to do some ranching in the far West. Overtaken by a storm, his livestock was all killed during the freeze. He had brought at the same time a sawmill. So he then decided to make a contract with the railroads then being built to go into the forest and cut railroad ties. So that was how he got started in the West, making railroad ties. He became eventually quite a well-known person in Wyoming, and a landowner. We're talking my great-grandfather, my grandmother's father.
DUNHAM: Yeah. Now, you mentioned the Civil War. Are you any relation of General Robert E. Lee?
LEE: No, I'm very definitely not a relation to this. I have to admit that my family is the Lee family from southern New Jersey. They were very well-known there, as glassmakers. They're in the area around Cape May, where they're glassmakers. I was told that even before my great-grandfather, that one of his relatives was one of those that organized a small inn. George Washington supposedly slept there on his way to Washington. Or going, actually, to New York.
DUNHAM: That's some history.
LEE: We have a family history that goes back at least to the family coming over from Europe. So anyway, my grandfather, a surveyor, was the very first of the surveyors in the Wyoming Territory, apparently. My father wanted us to see America, so when I was still fifteen he gathered the three of his younger children--my oldest sister decided she wanted to stay here and get married; she was five years older.
So anyway, the other three of us, we traveled by car from San Francisco up to the mouth of the Columbia River there, which is called Astoria. In Astoria, we had an automobile accident, you see. So we went to Astoria because we were visiting a friend of my father's, who was a lumberjack foreman. So my brother, myself, and my father, during the accident--my mother and sister moved into a small camp house, you might say--my father, my brother, and myself moved into the lumber camp. While we were there, we observed the cutting down of the original forest there. They were clear cutting.
DUNHAM: This is about '36? This is the summer of '36?
LEE: It was 1937.
DUNHAM: In the summer?
LEE: The summer of 1937, yeah, we went up there. It was a very interesting experience. Following that, when the car was repaired, we then went on to Wyoming, and actually to Idaho. Went to Lewiston, Idaho, visited relatives, and went down to Pocatello, and spent a month down there. I had my sixteenth birthday in Pocatello. Meantime, we took a side trip. My father and mother and an aunt who lived there in Pocatello, we went to Fort Bridger, in Wyoming. It was at that point that I was shown--under glass, displayed in the museum in Fort Bridger, which I understand is still there--my grandfather's surveys, survey slips of that Wyoming Territory. He then went on and moved over to--I think he went back to Evanston, where this pretty girl who became my grandmother was living with her father, and married her. And then he became the court clerk, the first court clerk for Evanston town, Wyoming. Sister went up years ago and was shown the original court books that he was in charge up.
DUNHAM: Was your grandfather still alive at the time of this trip?
LEE: No, he had passed on in 1890. Here's an aside for you. I tell my grandchildren, when I was twenty years old my president was FDR, right? When my father was twenty years old--he had just come to Hawaii here--his president was Theodore Roosevelt. When my grandfather was twenty years old, his president was Abraham Lincoln. Anyhow, as I say, that's an aside.
So anyway, getting back to--what happened is that my father, raised on a ranch that his grandfather had purchased near the town of Cokeville, in Wyoming--he was raised as a small boy, from that point. His father was nearby, and had borrowed a razor blade from the next-door neighbor, to shave his beard. Everybody had beards in those days. Grandfather didn't have one with him. So anyway, he borrowed this razor blade. Told my father, "Okay, I've shaved now; take this back to the owner." On the way back, my father, who was perhaps five or six years old, stopped to check out the razor, and broke a piece of it. So when his father, my grandfather, had found it out he took my father to the wood shed and, of course, gave him a good punishing--which is what they did in those days, I guess. A very few days later, my grandfather died, by, you might say, some sort of a--he, we think, was probably allergic to the chloroform that must've been used during the operation. He never recovered from the operation.
DUNHAM: What kind of operation was it?
LEE: Probably appendicitis or something similar, I don't know. It was supposedly a simple operation. He didn't even take his family down to help him, during the operation. We think the ether or chloroform, or what it might've been, that he was allergic to, and he went into a coma and died. So my father's last memory really of his father was this terrible spanking. A real whipping. He was still a small youngster.
DUNHAM: How did your grandmother make do, then, as a widow?
LEE: Well, she had several children to take care of. But then she moved back with the girls and some of the--my father's oldest brother was high school age, left high school and joined the railroad that went through Evanston, and went up and became a young, active engineer. Father became a helper on the ranch up in Cokeville. He remembers playing in the ruts of the--you see, at Cokeville some few years ago Cokeville had a centennial celebration where they had a celebration adjacent to the wagon tracks nearby, where the Oregon Trail separated from the California Trail, at Cokeville. But he remembers playing in the ruts of that wagon thing, you see. So as I say, in the West, that's a lot of that. So now my father is being raised on a ranch. It was a harsh life. So he decided he didn't want to become a cowboy. So as soon as he was able, after being a sheepherder and a roustabout and so forth, he went down to Portland and got a job in the shipping industry, Merchant Marine. So he became what is known as a wiper on a ship, which was traveling back and forth from California to Chile, loading guano, which was a fertilizer, to take back to the farms in California, a very messy job and a very tough job, but okay for a young guy.
DUNHAM: What doe a wiper do, exactly?
LEE: Well, more or less follows the instructions of the engineer, who takes care of the engine. Puts oil in things and stuff like that. Meanwhile, his mother, his two sisters--one older than he and one younger than himself--moved to Hawaii to join his mother's older brother, who had come to Hawaii. They tell me he had come to become one of the instructors at Punahou School. [Known also as Oahu College, it is now an exclusive private high school. President Obama was a graduate.] He had moved to Hawaii. All of a sudden everybody finds out that Hawaii's not a bad place to live. So little by little, the family moved, in most cases, to Hawaii. My grandmother, her two daughters. Before that, my father's two older brothers had come to also settle here and take jobs here. My uncle, Dad's oldest brother, became an engineer on a railroad, Mr. [Benjamin Franklin] Dillingham's railroad. So as soon as Father came here on his ship, which came here with a load of guano, he left the ship. He jumped ship. He'd had enough, you see. And he had a good reason to stay here. So then he joined the railroad. From the railroad--he was, by that time, in his early twenties, I guess.
DUNHAM: About what year would that have been?
LEE: That would've been about 1901, 1902, you see. The railroad started in Honolulu and went out and around Pearl Harbor, went out to Barbers Point; and then from there, went all the way to the Kaena Point, around Kaena Point, all the way to Kahuku, you see. And from Kahuku, there was yet another track, which went down as far as Kahana Bay. Did you know that there had been a railroad to Kahana Bay?
FUKUMOTO: No.
LEE: Yeah. And there was talk about taking it all the rest of the way around the island, but never happened, you see. But it was servicing the various plantations, you see. Up to that time, before the railroad, the way that the plantations sent their product in was by ship. The ship would go right to where they were. So anyway, Dad did that for a while. But Dillingham was the person who brought in the dredge to dredge both Pearl Harbor and Honolulu Harbor. So Dad decided, well, hey, that's not a bad job, so he then joined the dredging company. Quite a story on that, of course. But to make a long story short, Father was very successful in that, the dredge.
Eventually, he started dredging the channel into Pearl Harbor. Up to that time, it was known as the Pearl River. You see, both sides of the river converged on the other side of Ford Island, and then made the channel. Of course, where the channel went out, that's fresh water. So there was a channel there anyway. Coral won't grow in fresh water, you see. So that was used as the entry to Pearl Harbor. In addition, the Pearl Harbor dredge dredged the channel all the way around Ford Island. Dad, of course, was involved with that. In the meantime, he noticed that Pearl Harbor was being developed and thought, well, here's my chance to become a machinist--which was a fine profession in those days, of course. So he then switched to become a machinist in Pearl Harbor. And in the process, Father--this is still before the war; my oldest sister was born by then, and I think my brother was well on the way, three years older than myself. My brother has passed away, by the way.
DUNHAM: When did your father and mother meet, then?
LEE: So my father and mother met--she was quite young when he first came to the islands, and they met being neighbors. But I have a picture in there, incidentally, on the wall, showing my father in a bathing suit in what we call San Souci area of Waikiki. In those days, Waikiki was almost an isolated island, at one time, in the early 1900s. Oh, really, until the street cars came to Hawaii, or the power streetcars, Waikiki was known as a vacation area. Beaches and so forth.
DUNHAM: What part of the island were they neighbors, your mom and dad?
LEE: Well, they were neighbors in the middle town, Emma Street. My mother's family, in the meantime, her father, her grandfather and her great-grandfather moved to Hawaii from Tahiti. I can back up on that whole side of the family, if you wish.
DUNHAM: I would to know a little of the background there. Yeah, yeah, absolutely.
LEE: Okay, a little of the background. My great-grandfather from my mother's side was an English child born to parents who were somehow attending the court in Saint Petersburg, where he was born. My great-grandfather was born in Saint Petersburg, in Russia, from a family of English people who were part of the English representation in Russia. I don't know what the site was. But then he returned, being the youngest son of a fairly prominent family, as he grew up. Younger sons, in those days, in English families, joined one of the services, either the navy or the army. He joined the army, and became a young officer in one of the British armies. The army was sent to the Caribbean, and that was where this whole army was.
In the meantime, after our American Civil War, about that same time, the Maori in New Zealand had what they called an uprising. So this uprising created for the English population, which had built up in New Zealand by that time, a problem. So the British, from London, declared that that army will now move to New Zealand. In those days, the whole army moved. The army moved; the wives, the children, the dogs, the cats, and all the furniture was all shipped at one time. So that's what happened. So then the British Army went to New Zealand, fought with the New Zealand Maori, and eventually--the Maori were never really subdued, apparently, but there was a settlement. And in the process, my great-grandfather came to admire the Maori quite a bit. So when he decided to take his retirement in New Zealand, and settle there with his English family, he couldn't get along with the neighbors. So he said, "Well, the heck with that noise." So he picked his family up and moved.
He still liked the Polynesians, so then he moved to Tahiti. But in Tahiti, I'm not sure whether that particular family--I do know that the two sons of that family settled in Tahiti eventually; but I'm not sure about that great-grandmother, you see. But she might've settled there. But anyway, in the end there was either a divorce or a death in the family or such. So my grandfather, an older man at that time, married a very young Tahitian girl, see? Now they have three sons by this second marriage. He's an older fellow, quite a bit older than his bride, and he now has three sons to be raised in a French Catholic society. Uh-oh. He's an English Protestant, you see. So he found out that in Hawaii here, the Church of England actually had its representatives, through their Protestant missionaries here. So he picked his family up and came to Hawaii. So that's side of the island came here.
DUNHAM: So about when would that have been?
LEE: About 1870. Into 1870. He came with his three sons--one of them my grandfather--who were still children at that time.
DUNHAM: And his wife, his young wife.
LEE: And his young Tahitian wife, yeah, that's right. Then of course, they settled here in Hawaii, on Emma Street. Now, she's a Polynesian, and this was, of course, the Hawaiian monarchy in those days. She had some kind of relationship to Queen Pōmare in Tahiti. So the boys when they came here were allowed to attend the Royal School, where only the elite were allowed to attend. See, but the three boys attended Royal School, up there on Emma Street. So they settled near the school. Then at that time, ownership had to be in Polynesians' hands, so they established the Tahiti Lemonade Works. It was the first bottling works in Hawaii, as far as we know. I can show you a bottle, incidentally, here--
DUNHAM: Oh, that'd be great.
LEE: --of a type of bottle where there's no bottom to it. In other words, it's just rounded, you see. See? The old bottles were plugged with a leather plug. The way you got the product out of it--it was charged water, you see, or charged--. Anyway, the way was, you stomped the top of it and knocked the plug down, back into the bottle, and then you could pour out the contents. This was the way the bars supported it in those days. Because there were a lot of bars, of course, as your knowledge of Hawaii, I'm sure, is that Hawaii was settled first by some pretty rough people. One reason the missionaries came to Hawaii was to bring civilization to Hawaii, as they understood it. There's an old saying that they came to do good, and did quite well. So anyway, that's basically that side.
DUNHAM: But your mom was on the mainland, you said, when--or she lived on the mainland--
LEE: Well, she was actually born--well, what happened was that this bottling company needed updating, needing modernizing. So in order to do that, her grandfather, my great-grandfather, then picked the family up yet again. He had already become a citizen of Hawaii, you see, but then he picked up and went to the mainland, to learn the modern bottling works. Decided to become an American citizen. Someplace around, we have his--in California, he became a citizen of the United States. In the meantime, they learned the bottling business and moved back to their property here in Hawaii. My mother came back. She was born, as I say, and she came back as an infant, and grew up on the streets of Honolulu, near there, in what was a very nice area. The housing in that area, on Emma Street, is very close to the Pacific Club here in Honolulu, a very nice part of town still, the central part of town, just around the corner from the Queen's Hospital, for example, that area right in there.
My father moved to an apartment nearby, so he was actually acquainted with the family, including my mother, when she was still quite young. Maybe in her teens, early teens, you see. He was a good deal older by then. But for various reasons, eventually they--somehow, I'm not quite sure how, Waikiki gets into it. I think by the time this pretty young girl had moved to Waikiki. I think Dad was already living in Waikiki, in the San Souci area. So he then became friendly and things went to me. Had my older sister and my younger sister and my older brother, you see. So then getting to myself, I was born in the old Kapiolani Maternity Hospital, when it located on Beretania Street. Are you aware of that?
FUKUMOTO: Well, I'm aware of the location now.
DUNHAM: Yeah, the streets, but not the hospital.
LEE: Yeah, that's right, the location now. See, but it was moved up there. But of course it was still sponsored by the royal families, you see, just like Queen's was. And a couple of the others, also. But anyhow, children's hospital. Then what became the Shriners Hospital also was established by some member of the royal family. Princess Ruth perhaps, or something like that. Anyway, now I'm growing up. So my family moved--in the meantime, Father became a member of the--what do you call it? The workforce at Pearl Harbor.
DUNHAM: The civilian, but on the defense?
LEE: No, he left Mr. Dillingham's company and became what they call a--what's the term I'm thinking of?
DUNHAM: A defense worker?
LEE: No, wasn't a defense worker. It's when a government worker is called--a civil employee is what it is. Yeah, that's right. Anyway, so that's what he became.
DUNHAM: Yeah, my grandmother was for many years.
LEE: That's right. So anyway, then Dad had many Hawaiian friends. While he's working as a machinist, he and this friend of his went out to what is now Barbers Point. In those days, it was known as the Browns Camp; it was a camping area, where the fishermen went. The popular method of fishing in those days was to take a half a stick of dynamite, light it up, and throw it in the water, and the concussion killed the fish nearby. They would then float to the surface, and fellows waded out and harvested the fish. This was common, you see. So Dad, of course, held onto the dynamite stick too long, and it blew his hand off. You see? Now, he's a machinist, so now it's quite a harrowing time for him, because he then had to be taken back. The story is that they then had to amputate further up his arm, you see, and then sear that with a hot iron. That was the procedure in those days. It was done at Waipahu, at a plantation hospital. So there was no anesthetic. So he tells his children horror stories about that one, as you can imagine.
But anyway, he lived through it. Then finally he's recovering, with his remaining left arm. His left hand complete, and then his portion--it was about halfway up his right arm. Then he became very, very proficient at using what was left of his arm. He really was very proficient, but he could not be a machinist. So he didn't return to his job at Pearl Harbor. However, when he is still in recovery, he's with my mother--at that time, they're married with two children--and he's swimming at Waikiki. On those days, the naval officers from Pearl Harbor took their days off at Waikiki; there was a nice beach there. So he had been working for--well, they called him the captain of the yard. I don't remember what it title was, actually, but they called him the captain of the yard. So he's sitting on the beach talking to my mother. He's thinking of becoming an insurance agent and that sort of thing; what shall I do next?
Up the beach came the captain of the yard and his family, who knew my father quite well and had quite an admiration for his work ethic. Stopped and said, "How come you haven't returned to your job?" Dad said, "Well, I can't be a machinist anymore, you see, because as you could see, I've lost an arm." Or a hand, anyway, you see. So the captain of the yard, the chief naval officer in Pearl Harbor at that time, said, "Be in my office on Monday. I have a job for you." Unheard of now, of course, but in those days, he had a great admiration for my dad. So when Father went out to Pearl Harbor that following Monday morning--I think they went out on either trains or somehow or other got to Pearl Harbor--he was then placed as one of the workers at the Aiea Naval Water Pumping Station. They had just established a water pumping station to water Pearl Harbor. So my father was then one of those who was placed in that job--still a civil service job; that's what it is, civil service, civil service job--with the blessings of the captain of the yard, to become one of the workers. Then of course, he then became very proficient, and a few years later, was made in charge of the water supply for Pearl Harbor. That became his job. Then there was a house built on the area above the water pumping station, and that became our quarters, our navy quarters, so we moved in there. At that time, my parents had bought a house in Kaimuki, out behind Diamond Head area, you see.
DUNHAM: Now, had your mother's father passed?
LEE: By that time?
DUNHAM: Your grandfather. Did you ever know your grandfather, your maternal grandfather?
LEE: Oh, yeah. Oh, absolutely, knew him very well. Oh, yes.
DUNHAM: How old did he live to be?
LEE: Oh, he lived to be in his seventies. He had quite an interesting time, too, because when he was a young man--he grew up in Honolulu District, as I say, before the turn of the century, really. Did you know that there was a Honolulu opera company? They had their own entertainment here, in those days. Have you ever heard of Abner Doubleday?
DUNHAM: Yeah. The publisher?
LEE: No, Abner Doubleday was the man who devised the rules for baseball.
DUNHAM: Oh, yes! Okay, yes.
LEE: Okay? A very well-known person. He's buried up in Aloha Cemetery. He came to Hawaii, after he'd done all that stuff in New York. Settled in Hawaii here, became the fire chief.
DUNHAM: Is that part of why baseball's so popular here?
LEE: Yeah. One good reason, yeah. One good reason. But anyway, his gravesite is up there. Okay. Anyway, as I say, Honolulu became, for this part of the world, a very modern city, really, for the time. So we had streetcars, we had a telephone, we had electric lights--all these things, you see.
DUNHAM: And the opera company.
LEE: And the opera company. My grandfather was all part of that, see?
DUNHAM: As a performer, or--?
LEE: Yeah, he actually performed in some operas. So he became well known. And then eventually, he got into politics. By the time I knew my grandfather at first, he was the court clerk for Kailua Kona, over on the Big Island of Hawaii. So he was the court clerk over there, and had settled there with my grandmother. By that time, his two daughters had married and settled in here in Hawaii. So every summer, from the time I was approximately four years old or so, we were put on the Hualālai or the--what was the other one? Anyway, one of the little inner island boats, and shipped off to live spend the summers in Kailua Kona, see? Kailua Kona was the shipping point for the Parker Ranch, to ship its cattle down to Hawaii, Honolulu, to be slaughtered and used here, see? So anyway, it was quite an active little town. I'm sure if you've been there since, you've seen it's been very active. But in those days, it was a little village, seaside village. But anyway, he settled there, until his younger daughter died, and he then came back into Honolulu, to take care of his daughter's family, you see. That's my mother's sister's family. They had four children. So my mother had four children; her sister had four children, too, you see. Those four children were raised pretty much by the grandparents, here in Hawaii.
DUNHAM: Why was that, that they were mostly raised by the grandparents?
LEE: Oh, Honolulu was the place to be, for younger people. Here's where the schools were, here's where the businesses and so on. But grandfather came back and became a caretaker in the city hall, among other things. So he settled over there. Now, in the meantime, I'm growing up on the shores of Pearl Harbor. I had a very, very interesting time, too, because my neighbors were mostly plantation kids. Except that close to us, there was a pier that projected out into Pearl Harbor, which incidentally, is still there. The Navy used it, from time to time, as a landing for ships that were anchored in the harbor. But mostly it was used by the local fishermen, to bring their catches in. In those days, there were very many fishermen, mostly of Japanese extraction, coming with their twenty-six-foot Funai sampans. They were the sampan design, very, very seagoing, very stable boats. The Funai boat builders turned out a great number of these, with one lone diesel engine. Every night these sampans would go out into the ocean, through the Pearl Harbor entrance, and they would bottom fish out there, coming in in the early morning bringing their fish in to be placed on a train coming through from the west, all this way. Each of the fishermen had a box, which formerly held two ten-gallon cans of coal oil. Because you see, there was no electricity in those days in the fishing camps, so they used coal oil for their stoves, or what you call kerosene. But they saved these boxes. And these boxes, every morning, would be placed on the train, sent into the city. And in there, there was an Asian who'd pick up the boxes. Each one had the name of the fisherman on it. These fish were then sold, and then the agent there kept the family going. This was a very, very established way of doing business, you see.
DUNHAM: Yeah. These were a lot of your classmates' or playmates' parents?
LEE: A lot of my playmates' parents, right. Thank you.
DUNHAM: What was the ethnic makeup of your classroom?
LEE: Pretty well mixed. Because by the time I was into, first of all, grade school, my mix was--well, we had Caucasian kids, because very early on I started coming to the city to go to what was known as an English Standard School. We had English Standard Schools. The English Standard School for that side of the island was the Kapalama School, you see. So okay, that's fine, English Standard. The rule on the English Standard School was that if your language in your house was English, that you then were sort of automatically into English Standard. But with our large Oriental population--Filipino, Chinese, Japanese, and then not too many Koreans--they settled in Wahiawa. But also Portuguese. Portuguese was another part of the plantation thing. Anyway, the plantation workers, their kids went to the local schools, and were taught in a more rudimentary English. But to my way of thinking, I don't believe they were shortchanged on their education. I hear stories about the so-called separate-and-equal thing in the South, but I can really believe--gosh, all my schoolmates became doctors, lawyers. But what they did is when they went to U of H [University of Hawaii]--for example, the first semester at the U of H had to be in English indoctrination, you see. So a lot of these kids went to U of H. Perhaps you remember that. You don't remember the English Standards?
FUKUMOTO: I'm trying to think, just from my parents' experience growing up in Kauai. It was a little different, though.
LEE: Did they go to U of H?
FUKUMOTO: My mom went for a year, actually, and then had an opportunity.
LEE: Well, if you ever get a chance, ask her if she didn't get one semester of what they call English--.
DUNHAM: Yeah, that's a good question.
FUKUMOTO: That's a really good question. I wouldn't be surprised if she did, yeah.
LEE: Yeah. Well, that's because, as I say--and yet our schoolteachers, even in the country schools--I went to AIEA School for a couple of years, too. Our schoolteachers came from a lot of different states, on contract, came to Hawaii. They were placed in houses on the school property. At all these country schools, you see, the transportation was limited, so these schoolteachers were placed in these properties. I've often been told I don't have an accent, because my schoolteachers, almost universally, came from areas all over the US, especially central US and the western US.
DUNHAM: So they were all Caucasian, all the teachers that came?
LEE: So they were all mostly Caucasian, yeah. At the same time, however, we were establishing--teachers college at U of H has been there for a long time, hasn't it? I don't know when it got started.
FUKUMOTO: I don't know.
LEE: Well, my oldest cousin was one of the first attendees at the U of H, which had been established in the early 1920s. That was my cousin {Benjie?}.
DUNHAM: Studied education, or studied teaching?
LEE: No. Well, his father had a business here, so he joined his father's business.
DUNHAM: So the students that you were in school with at the English school, your classmates, their parents were not working on the plantations then, is that right?
LEE: Some were.
DUNHAM: Oh, some were.
LEE: Some were, yeah, yeah. Because, well, Bobby Fukuda, for example. Close friend. His parents were émigrés from Japan. But one thing about the people that came from the Orient, they were super, super interested in education. Believe me, those kids, they not only went to American school, but after that, they went to Japanese school, for example. So education was very big with those folks. Much more so than we were, really.
DUNHAM: Yeah. So if they didn't speak English as their primary language in the home--
LEE: They learned it fast.
DUNHAM: --they could still apply to go to the English school?
LEE: Oh, absolutely. Absolutely. Absolutely. To make it perfectly clear, my mother was educated in McKinley High School. That was not a so-called English Standard, in those days, you see? You see? Thank you. But her companions were, again, the kids from Honolulu. McKinley High School was established in honor of President McKinley, very early on. So that was her graduation, from there. My father graduated from the third grade, he said, in Wyoming. After that, his mother--his father passed away. Up there, that was instant poverty. So she moved back to her family home in Evanston, with some of the folks. But the boys were put to work almost immediately.
DUNHAM: I think I read something of a short article with you, where you talked about the Caucasian teachers, and struggling with some of your classmates' names. Could you tell us about that?
LEE: Oh, yeah. You read that, huh? My brother's business partner was a guy named Roy Kuwasaki. And his brother was Rex Kuwasaki. Rex became the president of von Hamm-Young. But he was born and raised in Pahoa, in the far Hawaii. His brother Roy said, "We all have Japanese names," which they all do. Their grandchildren don't even know, as I can tell you. But anyway, as Roy told me, he said, one day his teacher, who came from someplace in California, perhaps, or something like that, lined all the boys up on one side of the room and the girls on the other side of the room, of this teaching room out in Paauilo there. Said, "Okay, now your name is Roy and your name is Rex and your name is John." She went right down the line, and these kids, by and large, adopted those names, you see. In the case of my friends, one of the fishing families adjacent to me there in Aiea. Very close, very close. So much so their mother--do you know what the term picture bride is? Have you heard the term picture bride? Well, Mrs. Chagami, who stood maybe a full five feet tall, maybe, and has at least ten children, she was a picture bride. She came to Hawaii and--
DUNHAM: Was that openly known?
LEE: Oh, of course. Oh, absolutely. No, there were agents in Japan, sending pictures to these gentlemen living in Hawaii there.
DUNHAM: I know it occurred; I didn't know if people tried to be discreet about it.
LEE: Oh, no. Yeah, no, no. Well, as I say, I don't know that it was a big deal. But anyway, in the case of Mr. Chagami--who was really a great guy, he really was, rough and tough, but he was a great guy--he used to take my brother and myself out on his fishing boats, and we'd go overnight. My mother trusted him perfectly. So we went out on his boats, and we'd spend overnight one time. We wouldn't go together. Jimmy would go and I would go. I think he did it to help him keep awake. But he'd bottom fish all night long. The boats had water compartments in them, and as he pulled his fish aboard, he unhooked them and threw them right into these little water tanks built into the boat. Keep them fresh, you see. So when he came in in the morning, as soon as he docked, he would take all these fish out, put them in his little box and send them off to Honolulu.
DUNHAM: Right, right. And what all was he catching?
LEE: Bottom fish. All these Hawaiian names. Opalu and Menpachi.
FUKUMOTO: So did you love fishing yourself? Did you grow up fishing all the time?
LEE: Well, yeah, I like fishing. I hated hunting. I went hunting a couple of times on Mauna Kea, but I just really disliked it.
DUNHAM: What did you dislike about hunting?
LEE: I didn't like the shooting of the animals. As I say, my dog is in there. I have a dog and two cats, and I love them and so forth. But anyway, no, I'm sorry we diverge so much about--
DUNHAM: Oh, no, it's very interesting.
LEE: But anyway, Mrs. Chagami--they had basically what you would call a shack to live in, right next to the water there. But you could eat off the floor, I'll tell you. It was just beautiful. But at night, they put down tatami mats to sleep on, and in the morning they'd roll them up and put them aside and so forth. And they cooked on a kerosene stove. My gosh, we had an electric stove in our place. Wow! Anyway, one day we two boys--her son and I were the same age; we were just a few days apart. Like small boys--we were all really small--we're squabbling back and forth, and I'm saying, "Oh, you Japanee," and, "Oh, you haole." [laughs] We're talking back and forth, because he was--. The door opens up, and out comes Mrs. Chagami. She says, "Hey." She points at me and she says, "Now you're Masaru." And she points to her son and says, "Now you're Robert." So to that day, she called me Masaru. She did. So through there, many years after that, that I was Masaru. Oh, I remember that so well. She was a dear lady.
I had a real problem when I moved to Michigan--this is during the war, and of course, up there, it was all Jap this and Jap that--so I said, "Hey, wait a minute." I said, "We never use the term in Hawaii." I said, "Besides, I've got what amounts to a mother, a second mother, who's actually Japanese." I couldn't make these guys understand that. I shut up after a while; I didn't say anything about it. But actually, I told the fellows that I was in there [with], I said, "Listen, hey, you knock it off." But they got to know that I just wasn't going to go along with all that jazz. And yet I can assure you that at the time of the attack, I was enormously angry at the Japanese, I can assure you.
DUNHAM: We'll talk more about that and the kind of complexity of that in a little bit. But we have a little more time left on this tape.
LEE: Oh, I'm sorry.
DUNHAM: Oh, no, you're doing great. It's so fascinating. As long as you've got time today.
LEE: Well, anyway.
DUNHAM: But I wanted to know, so you're a young boy coming; and then I'm curious in Hawaii, how the Depression impacted your family and/or the community in general.
LEE: Well, civil service, in those days, was a wonderful place to be. My father and mother had civil--well, my father had civil service privileges. At that time, he could buy food at the navy commissary in Pearl Harbor. He had a very basic civil service job, when you get right down to it, but we kids never missed a meal. However, our parents got up quite early in the morning to make sure that we were taken care of. You could talk all you want about the greatest generation, but to me, that greatest generation was the people that actually kept things going through the Depression. I have you know. They were wonderful, really.
DUNHAM: Yeah, enormous strength.
LEE: It was the same thing, all the plantation families. You realize, gosh almighty, we were just hanging in there, but we kids didn't feel it. We didn't miss meals. I can remember that one of my sandwiches that I took to school--in Kapalama we didn't have a kitchen; everybody had to bring their own lunch in those days--but I can remember having what was known as a relish spread sandwich. That was bread with a very liberal coating of relish in it. There's no meat. That was it. But that got us through. Of course, when we got home, we had--my mother cooked a lot with what is known as salt pork. This is a common farm thing, where it's just preserved pork, and you have to boil it, to boil the salt off of it, before you can prepare it. But we had a lot of those meals, as such. My father, as I say, on his ranch, he had learned how to cook, too. So he was great on baking biscuits. Cereal in the morning, biscuits, and stuff like that, milk. Yeah, sure.
DUNHAM: Of course you have your Japanese, and probably friends of other ethnicities. Did you eat at their families, and/or did their cultures and foods come into your own kitchens?
LEE: Oh, sure. Yeah, yeah. Well, at one time, in Aiea there, we had a hurricane that went up to that side of the island, the western side of the island. There was a tremendous hurricane. Aiea was pretty well washed out. So my mother, who had many friends there, went down. She brought back a half a dozen families to live in the house, because we had a toilet, we had cooking facilities, we had running water. So my brother and I slept in one of our clothes closets. We were small, so we slept in the clothes closet. There were folks sleeping everywhere. But the house stood the storm quite well. But a lot of the flooding that went on down at the lower lands was what--but my mother had friends down there and she brought them back. Of course, it dried out pretty quickly and the folks went back; but I can remember two or three days when our houses were filled like that. People in those days took care of each other quite well. You don't see that kind of cross-dependence these days. Well, you certainly still do.
DUNHAM: Your mother's parents, your grandparents on that side, how old did they live to be?
LEE: Well, let's see. Grandfather lived to about one--he was living with on December 7.
DUNHAM: Oh, he was still with you then?
LEE: Perhaps I should get into the December 7 thing.
DUNHAM: Well, maybe let's wait till the next tape on that. We have just a few minutes left on the tape. We'll take a short break.
LEE: Oh, okay, yeah. Well, I attended school in Aiea School for two years. I was not what you'd call a good student. So Mother brought me home, and my sister and myself attended the Aiea School, this non-English standard School. Had a wonderful experience, with great friends.
DUNHAM: What grades are these, then?
LEE: With me, it was the sixth and seventh grades. Yeah, that's right, sixth and seventh grades. Finally, I decided I would go to Roosevelt, with my older sister and my older brother, so they bought me a pair of shoes. So that was the first time I wore shoes to school.
DUNHAM: Did you ever wear shoes, to that point?
LEE: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah, sure. I had to be known as having shoes. [laughs] No, but I had a very proper American family, of course. My father's younger sister, my Aunt Florence, was a wonderful, wonderful woman. I never knew the older sister. She married and moved away before I was ever aware of her. And passed away, incidentally. But then the younger sister, my Aunt Florence, a maiden aunt, never married. But she was wonderful to us kids. She really was.
DUNHAM: Can I ask, too, with your Tahitian family background, was that something that was pronounced? Or the Tahitian culture and/or the Hawaiian culture then?
LEE: It was a kind of a cross culture. The Tahitian side, well, my grandfather, even though he came here as a young boy, it was always Waititi. He had a lot of Tahitian things like that. For example, the Hawaiian word for A'amanu. What is the Tahitian word for bird? They're very similar. I forget what it is now. The word for island, for example, is moku. Then in Tahiti, it's motu. You see? So they can talk back and forth.
DUNHAM: But you were identified as Haole, not as--
LEE: Oh, absolutely. Yeah, yeah.
DUNHAM: Your mother and her siblings, how were they identified?
LEE: Haole.
DUNHAM: Even though they were half--
LEE: Yeah, because my grandfather, before Mother came back, as an infant, he, and two brothers, as well, had married girls from California. So my grandmother was a girl who was actually a California girl. The family, at that time, settled in Riverside, in California. The university there--Redlands, I think now; I don't know what it was in those days, but--that's where the boys went to school and met these girls, all three of them Haole girls, to bring back to Hawaii.
DUNHAM: Wait, am I right? Your mom's mother was Tahitian? No? Who was Tahitian? How far back did that go?
LEE: My mom's grandmother was pure Tahitian. Except that her maiden name was {Dutallier?}. But you see, in Tahiti, they have what they call a matriarchal society. Yeah, okay, fine. So any girl that pops out of a Tahitian girl, any person that pops out of a Tahitian girl, is Tahitian.
DUNHAM: Interesting. So it's where you're born--
LEE: In other words, the genealogy--so my great-grandmother's birth certificate was not given to her until she was in her thirties. See? After that time, we went back and researched it. The French are very meticulous. So fine, she went back to get her birth certificate. She either went back or sent back--I forget now--to go and get her birth certificate, showing that she was--she wanted to be descended from the Pōmare family, you see, Pōmare line. But when she went back there, they say, "Yeah, yeah, your mother had this Pōmare thing." But her father proved to be a French naval officer named {Dutallier?}. You see? [laughs] So they said--but as far as everybody was concerned, she's Tahitian.
DUNHAM: And as you said, when they first came here with the three children, they got to go to the Royal School.
LEE: The Royal School. The Royal School, yeah.
DUNHAM: Based on being Tahitian?
LEE: Based on the Tahitian genealogy. Yeah, that's right, that's right.
DUNHAM: Interesting.
FUKUMOTO: It's powerful.
DUNHAM: We're at the end of this tape; why don't we take a short break?
DUNHAM: It's tape two, on July 26, 2014, with Robert Lee. You were just talking about sort of your middle school, I think, sixth and seventh grade. Was there anything else you wanted to share about kind of those years?
LEE: Oh, no. At the time, at Aiea School, I was the only Caucasian boy, and my sister was the only Caucasian girl in the school.
DUNHAM: Oh, what was that like?
LEE: Oh, perfectly great. I developed friends, which I kept the rest of my life, at that time, too.
DUNHAM: Did you get made fun of at all, as a Haole, though? Did you get made fun of or picked on?
LEE: Yeah, sure.
DUNHAM: So that was hazing, if you will?
LEE: Sure. But the thing is about that, that life in the Aiea plantation was--I don't recall any real maliciousness or anything like that. Since that time, you can hear, they robbed us, they stole our land, all that sort of stuff; but I didn't encounter any of that, really. As a matter of fact, my family--I think I mentioned to you earlier, this case where this old Hawaiian man--he was an old-time Hawaiian. He was as big as your pictures of Duke Kahanamoku, for example. Incidentally, the Duke was a friend of the family, too.
DUNHAM: You're talking about the fisherman with the big hands?
LEE: The fisherman with the big hands.
DUNHAM: You just told us about that off camera, but maybe you want to share that.
LEE: No. Well, anyway, old man {Kaleo?}, who had the fishing rights for Kahuku Point, as long as he was alive, the property remained his. But when he was to pass on, then the property reverted to the--I think it's the James Campbell estate. They're the ones that have built the hotel that's out there, and the golf courses and various things. That's all so-called James Campbell property now. Campbell was an early--really, a pioneer developer here in Hawaii. He's the one that was well known for finding the water in the Ewa Plain, for example, and drilling down; that was one of his things. So anyway, that estate is still very well established, and there are very many descendants now, too.
DUNHAM: Right. But what you had said was that in that case, at least, they got the land was by starting to pay the taxes for the land.
LEE: By finally paying the taxes, in a lot of the cases, yeah. I really am not the authority on how that all happened, and as I say, a lot of mine is hearsay, of course. Anyhow, at the same time that that was happening, My father became friends, my mother and father, rather, became friends with the {Helenihi?} family in Hawaii. So one of the things that happened was that the property that is now developed into what is called Iwilei was actually in the name of the {Helenihi?} family. But it was hot, dry land, and the developer of the pineapple plantations came to the family--this is, of course, hearsay again--and said, "I have just bought the property inside the Honolulu Harbor, which whenever the tide comes up, the fish come in, and it's really a great place to catch mullet and so forth. Let's trade." So that sounded like a good deal, so there was a trade made between that in the Honolulu Harbor property and the property in Iwilei. Then the pineapple cannery was constructed. As the story goes, the owner--I think his name was Joseph, if I'm not mistaken--passed away, and his wife became the heir to the property. Then she went to, among other people, my aunt and my father, and said, "How do I keep this property? What do I do with it, this property in Honolulu Harbor?" They said, "Well, let's go to a lawyer and have a title made."
So they did, they went and they took title to that property inside the harbor, right? Just at that same time, a very fine gentleman named Mr. Dillingham, who had previously brought in the dredges and so forth, bought property in Honolulu Harbor, further on out, around the Pier 32 area now. Then he brought his dredge into Honolulu Harbor and made a contract with the city--or the state or somebody, probably--to dredge Honolulu Harbor and make a working harbor out of it. So he dredged the property. But then he was going to construct a pipeline and pump all of this debris, that came out as dredgings, over to the property inside the harbor, that he had just bought. In other words, this was all the same thing; very low land, the fish come in and this whole thing.
But to get his pumping rights through to his property, he had to go over the Helenihi land, you see? So Mrs. Helenihi said, "Yeah. You certainly can bring your pipes of debris across my land, but you've got to fill my land first." So he did. Mr. Dillingham, a very fine person, really. I've met him; I knew who he was. He said yes, he'll do that. So he then used the fill from the first parts of Honolulu Harbor to fill the Helenihi property inside Honolulu Harbor, to make land out of it. And these lands then became Piers 24, 25, 26, right in that corner of property. And then he went on to fill his own land with the rest; with actually, most of the dredgings.
DUNHAM: How long did they keep that land then?
LEE: Well, the thing is that one of the great friendships in my parents' family, with that family, is that my father insisted that Mrs. Helenihi, the wife, work up an ironclad lease through the lawyers. The lawyers that Dad knew were--well, as I say, my aunt happened to be a legal secretary. So she went right to her boss, who was one of the local judges, and they made up this ironclad lease. Then in the meantime, the cannery needed a can making place. So they were in such a rush that the canning manufacturer rushed out to Hawaii and made a sort of a handshake agreement to build the can company, the can making factory side-by-side with the cannery, with Mr. Dole's cannery, you see? As I say, on sort of a handshaking agreement. Then lo and behold, it turned out to be very successful. So now okay, we're taking over your property, Mrs. Helenihi. We've got this deal, see? No. So to this day, the Helenih family still retains that property. I do know that one friend of mine--{Sonny?}, we call him; one of the sons, incidentally, one of her sons, one of {Mary Ann's?} sons, was named after my father. His name was Alfred. They were, as I say, a very close relationship. Mother and Mrs. Helenihi were very close friends.
DUNHAM: Because your family played as pivotal role in helping them keep the property, whereas so many lost.
LEE: So many lost, yeah. So it's quite a valuable piece of property now. It's the piers there, and also it is the canning company, which is now--they don't make cans anymore, but it's a great big building there, you see, which is a business building and so forth. So all of these rentals and so forth still are retained in the Helenihi family descendants.
DUNHAM: So they just lease all the--.
LEE: They lease it all, yeah. A couple of attempts have been made to break the lease, but it was done too well.
DUNHAM: Yeah. Well, good.
LEE: Isn't that cute?
DUNHAM: Yeah.
FUKUMOTO: That's a great story.
DUNHAM: It's important. Yeah, well, back to the school, when you said you and your sister were the only Haole. How about among other ethnic groups there? Are there some that sort of got along or bonded more than others, or had challenges?
LEE: Yeah. Yeah, yeah. My particular friends were pretty much the Japanese group. The Filipinos were later in coming to Hawaii than the Japanese. But gosh, my next door neighbor over here is of that original group, too. I hope he's still alive; he has been very ill. But anyway, that sort of thing, you see. A lot of those plantation folks were very simple people. We were very simple people, too, I can assure you.
DUNHAM: And then where did you go to high school?
LEE: Then in high school, I started with the eighth grade, and went to Roosevelt High School, which in those days, actually was a six-year school. That was before the middle school was established, you see.
DUNHAM: Okay. So you were starting not in the first year of its school.
LEE: No, no.
DUNHAM: It was sixth through twelfth?
LEE: Yeah, well, six through twelve would've been our so-called primary school. I mean our main school. No, I'm sorry. Seventh through twelfth is the so-called high school.
DUNHAM: You started in the second year of it, essentially.
LEE: So I started in the second year, in the eighth grade there. Yeah, that's right. I'm sorry.
DUNHAM: So that was more--?
LEE: Oh, very great. That was wonderful, you see. I was quite happy there. Had a lot of friends there, a lot of my schoolmates there, of course.
DUNHAM: There're more Caucasians in Roosevelt?
LEE: Oh, more so. More Caucasians, but by no means all.
DUNHAM: Right, right.
LEE: {Kia Rakona?} was a good friend, and as I say, {Bobby Fukudo?}. And another one that you may encounter yet was Ted Tsukiyama. Ted Tsukiyama is another one that works with Paul.
DUNHAM: Okay. So he's still around, and maybe we should try to talk to him, yeah.
LEE: But then in that school, also we had a lot of mixed Hawaiian, a lot of Portuguese. Lots of Chinese kids, of course.
DUNHAM: Were Chinese working in the plantations, or what were they mostly doing?
LEE: I think then, they were pretty well out of the plantations, yeah.
DUNHAM: Late thirties? Yeah.
LEE: They had become entrepreneurs, really. It's kind of cute. Now, for example, we have a company in town that's a very well-known furniture company, the C.S. Wo Furniture Company. Right? Actually, that family belongs to the Ching family. The family is known as the Ching Sing Wo family. Wo is the second name of Mr. Ching. However, in putting up the signs, the last name Wo, well, okay, just like it is in English. So the family's actually changed their name legally to Wo, you see. But they're still members of the Ching clan, believe me. They're nice people, let me tell you. Very good people.
DUNHAM: And in the thirties when you're in high school, are you following news at all of the war in the Pacific with Japan, what's going on with them?
LEE: Well, no. Actually, at that time, it was pretty well established that our competition in the Pacific was Japan. I can go back through various stories about how the Japanese wanted to take over Hawaii. Actually, a large part of the reason Hawaii became independent was that it was said that they wanted to marry a Japanese royal prince to the princess of Hawaii, being Princess Ka'iulani. Princess Ka'iulani had been educated in England, and had fallen in love with an English person. Her father, incidentally, was of English descent, but had married a queen here. Okay? I think Lili'uokalani, who incidentally, lived up the hill here. Anyway, when she was literally forced to come back to Hawaii, before the turn of the century, and leave her beloved English fellow back in England, to come home to be married to this Japanese prince, that it broke her heart and she then soon afterwards, passed away. Before Mama ever came back to Hawaii, that had happened. It was still in the 1890s, when she passed on, I'm sure. So the other Hawaiian royalty that was here were pretty well persuaded by the local sugar planters and local businesses to go along with the idea of becoming, first of all, a territory of Hawaii. And the little booklet between so-called incorporated territories, of which Hawaii was one, and the unincorporated territories, like Samoa and Guam, different status. In fact, I had Paul give me a--I have it in a place there, actually--a difference in the various territorial statuses of the--but anyway, Hawaii was to be a territory. What's the term I said?
FUKUMOTO: Incorporated?
LEE: An incorporated territory, that's right, yeah.
DUNHAM: So to what extent, as teenagers, are you or any of your peers, of whatever ethnicity, aware of--?
LEE: We were Americans.
DUNHAM: But to what extent are you aware of what's going on in Japan, and Japan breaching its outreach?
LEE: Well, actually, not very much, really. Not very much. Except, of course, that my barber--incidentally, in those days, I could get a haircut for ten cents.
DUNHAM: Very nice.
LEE: In Aiea town. Finally one day the barber said, "Hey, you're getting to be pretty old. I'm going to start charging you a quarter." [laughs] But anyway, that barber had a picture of the Japanese fleet, the Japanese fleet of ships. Now, we're talking now about after the World War I, when the Japanese really became prominent in the Pacific, you see? They had an enormous fleet of ships, of various types of warships. All of them were pictured on his wall there, because he was a very loyal Japanese man. I think he was one of those who was taken in, as a suspected--
DUNHAM: Incarcerated right after--
LEE: Incarcerated, right, thank you. Thank you. Of course, you know the story on the memorial of Pearl Harbor; you know that the design there was by [Alfred] Preis.
DUNHAM: We know a little bit about it, from the tour, mostly.
LEE: Well, Preis was also an enemy alien. But after we were convinced that he really wasn't a believer in Hitler, why, he was let out, and then he went back to his original business of being a very efficient and very well-known architect.
DUNHAM: Yeah, interesting. Yeah. Well, what else about your high school years, then?
LEE: Well, actually, I have some annuals in there. In my senior year, I was one of the ROTC officers.
DUNHAM: Oh, you were?
LEE: I had a little ROTC troop behind me. One of my troop members became the president of First Hawaiian Bank, eventually, that sort of the things. So these kids grew up to become--in other words, we had a good education. I probably had as poor an education as anybody, because I had discovered surfing.
DUNHAM: Tell us about that. What was surfing like in those days?
LEE: Well, surfing was quite different than now. We would never have survived, with our kinds of boards, with the kind of ocean that the kids now surf in automatically. In other words, we would never have surfed in a surfboard off of, oh, a pipeline and stuff like that.
DUNHAM: The waves, they were like that then; you just didn't go there.
LEE: Oh, yes. North shore waves were still breaking like they do.
DUNHAM: You just weren't crazy. [laughs]
LEE: That's right. We wouldn't have dared to go out in that stuff, not with the boards we had. Duke Kahanamoku, for example, had a board that was about sixteen feet long. As a child, I was part of the little group in Waikiki. Duke Kahanamoku, in those days, was an older man. His board, he could surf it and he was a beautiful surfer, on that koa board. Sixteen feet of koa. And not light. He'd surf that board. Oh, he and the other surfing beach boys would surf their koa canoes, in the surf that was there, which was a pretty good surf. But it took two or three of us boys to help Duke get his board into the water. He had it stored in a locker. Nobody dared to bother his locker, believe me. But anyway, he would take it out, and then two or three of us youngsters would run up and say, "Yeah, Duke, we'll take it for you." We'd pick it up and take it out to the ocean. He'd walk along behind, with his arms folded, to go out and go surfing.
DUNHAM: How did you learn to surf? As a young boy, you did?
LEE: Actually, one of the beach boys taught me how to surf, on the wooden boards of those days. A fresh, new wooden board would surf very well; but we didn't have any skegs. My son has a board in there. But we didn't have the skegs, for example. They were of, more or less, a cigar shape. I had one here for many years, but finally burned it up in a fire someplace.
DUNHAM: So surfing was one of the distractions from high school?
LEE: Very much so, yeah.
DUNHAM: Maybe dating, too? We've talked openness with friendship, with dating.
LEE: Oh, yeah.
DUNHAM: Were there any issues with issues interracially?
LEE: No, no. I don't think we dated interracially much. That was as much to do with the Japanese parents as it was to do, for example, with the haoles. Yet we were friends. But believe me, if one of our nice little Japanese girls had brought home a Haole boy, as some of them started to do during the war--they started bringing the servicemen home--their parents just went out of their minds, really. Because in fact, one of the Chagami girls started dating a Korean boy. Wow. In fact, some of the very, very earlier ones had a problem with their kids dating Okinawan kids.
DUNHAM: Yeah. Candice is a mix of Okinawan, but she knows well about that, that history and the discrimination.
LEE: It was discrimination, that's right, you see? We knew about it, but we didn't get involved much.
DUNHAM: Do you remember any--?
LEE: Like I say, most of my dates were nice, easy, you might say, just kid dates, with other pretty little Haole girls.
DUNHAM: Did you ever have crushes on non-Haole girls, but know that you couldn't do anything about it, if you will?
LEE: Well, to the extent that some of those Hawaiian Hapa Haole a beautiful race; a beautiful race were the Chinese Hawaiian girls. Because the Hawaiians had the dark skin and rather voluptuous, and the Chinese kids were slim and pretty, had beautiful hair. Of course, Hawaiian girls have beautiful hair, too. But no, as I say, they certainly were beautiful kids, that's for sure. Our Hawaiian friends were good, too. But we just didn't have that much time.
Anyway, so my dates, right through high school, were with my schoolmates. Going back quite a bit, one of the groups of people that came to Hawaii early on, right after the Civil War, were the Portuguese. Most people don't know that. But the Portuguese first came to Hawaii as a group, from Ohio, where they had previously settled from New England, because they were fishermen, and they started fishing the Great Lakes. So the first Portuguese to come to Hawaii were not from the Azores; they were from Ohio. They settled in, and that's really--I don't know what the religion might've been then. Of course, very strongly, later on, the Portuguese became Catholics. More or less gravitated to Catholics. I'm sorry, did I move? I'm sorry.
DUNHAM: Oh, no. No worries, no worries, you're fine.
LEE: Another interesting group that came to Hawaii were the Negroes that came from the South. A very large group came to Hawaii. Very similar to Hawaiians, and they soon integrated with a lot of Hawaiian folks, you see? But some of them stayed, remained the same. One of my mother's close, close friends was Mrs. Nollie Smith. Nollie was the husband. Her name was Evelyn, I think. Her children and we became good friends. Nollie, a close friend of--but we were boys to boys and girls to girls. So my sister became a very close friend of Mrs. Smith's daughter, Evalani; I became a very close friend with Mrs. Smith's son, Nollie, Jr.
DUNHAM: Were they a mix of Hawaiian and black or they were--?
LEE: No, they were all black. Thank you.
DUNHAM: From the South originally.
LEE: But we regularly, when we went from Aiea, went into town, Mother had a visit with her close friend, Evelyn Smith.
DUNHAM: Yeah. And do you know what kind of work their father did?
LEE: Yeah, he was with the government. Eventually, he left Hawaii--I'm not sure whether they divorced or what the story was--but he eventually left Hawaii and became an administrator in the--what are the islands that we took over from--?
FUKUMOTO: In the Caribbean? Are you talking about Puerto Rico?
LEE: Not Puerto Rico, no, no. He became an administrator to the--we call them the Virgin Islands. He became very prominent over there, as a matter of fact. That was the dad. Nollie and all the kids stayed here. Nollie became a politician, Junior, as I say, on Maui, and so forth.
DUNHAM: Yeah. And the Portuguese you mentioned, so they mentioned that initial migration after the Civil War, from Ohio through here, as fisherman. During your time, were they fishermen? I've also heard managers of the plantations, a lot of times.
LEE: Some of them were, as I say; or they were various luna usually--a luna being a minor official. I live in {Luna Apono?} place, {Luna?} being a boss, you see. You talk about friction. The Filipinos were all from the Philippines. The idea of the plantation was that they had various camps for these various ethnic groups. They had the Japanese camp, they had the Chinese camp, they had the Filipino camp. In the case of the Filipino camp, of course, if you came to the Philippines, you went and you were put in the Filipino camp. Filipinos from Mindanao are Muslim. Filipinos from Ilocos Norte are Christian. You see? There was enormous friction between these two sects.
DUNHAM: Because they were just thrown in together?
LEE: They were thrown in together. There were a lot of internal problems, of all sorts, yeah. We remember it well because being next to the plantation, we saw a lot of that happening, you see.
DUNHAM: What about other culture, music? Did you grow up with different kinds of music? Or did you play any music yourself, or have friends--?
LEE: I didn't myself. I have a tin ear. But other members of the family played ukulele, for example, and guitar. String instruments, mostly. I was not successful as a horn [player], that's for sure, so I gave that up very early on. A couple of the family members play the piano quite well. But my sister is more or less an artist, my older sister. Some of her artwork, I have. My daughter, also, my oldest daughter, was quite good at art, too. So thank you. I have some of hers, too.
DUNHAM: But did you observe the music around you, kind of, and the mix of cultures?
LEE: I enjoyed it, yeah. Oh, yeah, sure.
DUNHAM: Or did you see that play out?
LEE: In those days, we had what we called Hapa-Haole music. The music of those days is quite different from what you hear now.
DUNHAM: Can you describe it for us?
LEE: Well, we were able to transition to swing very easily. Like for example, Johnny Noble. [sings] "I love a pretty Haole girl. She lives in Waikapu. Rosy cheeks and pearly teeth and lovely nut-brown hair." That sort of thing, you see? Nowadays, when you hear it, it's quite different. "Kill the Haole girl." [they laugh]
DUNHAM: You're saying things were less tense at that time, or less friction then?
LEE: No, but it was pretty well accepted. My Hawaiian music still enthralls me, really. I love it. But you hear so much of it. And yet a lot of it still harks back to that time. A lot of the players, too, of course.
DUNHAM: Yeah, there's still a lot of traditional playing.
LEE: Oh, sure, absolutely. But a lot more Hawaiian is spoken now than was spoken in those days. Much more so. We learned a lot of phrases, words, stuff like that, but the kids of today are learning the language. Which is quite good, I think. But a lot of what these kids are saying I don't understand at all.
DUNHAM: So you were surfing, and--did you finish high school?
LEE: Oh, yeah. Finished high school.
DUNHAM: Yeah, okay. So in '39?
LEE: No, I finished in 1940, because that year that we spent on the mainland was a year out of school for me.
DUNHAM: That was a whole year you traveled.
LEE: Yeah, we traveled for half a year. My brother came home to go to the university. I stayed on the mainland and attended school there; but when I came home, they didn't give me credits for anything there. So I attended a school for a semester in Oakland.
DUNHAM: Oakland, California.
LEE: California.
DUNHAM: Do you know what high school?
LEE: Yeah.
DUNHAM: Oakland Tech or--?
LEE: No, no, University. Uni.
DUNHAM: Where was it?
LEE: Berkeley. University High School. It was an offspring of Cal. It was in Berkeley, actually.
DUNHAM: Was it on Milvia? Because Berkeley High School is on Milvia now.
LEE: Yeah, yeah. No, no, it was down towards the waterfront. It's no longer there. University no longer exists, as such. It was closed out. All the girls had to wear uniforms. The boys could come as they were, more or less. But the girls--well, at that time there was the thing, as I recall, that so many of the girls were from affluent families from Oakland and other places, so they were dressing super-duper. Whereas other girls that were coming to school had to put on more plain clothing.
DUNHAM: So this was a way of addressing that.
LEE: So it was just, "Hey, let's do it." If I'd been smart--of course, that was just at the time when Longs Drugs was being built. The very first Longs drugstore was right there in Berkeley. If I'd been smart, I would've bought stock.
DUNHAM: Were you living on your own then, or you were with your mother?
LEE: Well, my mother stayed on. She and my sister and I stayed and lived in a place in Oakland, near some old friends of the family.
DUNHAM: I'm sorry to diverge, but since we're from there--
LEE: Oh, I see.
DUNHAM: Candice teaches and Oakland, and I work in Berkeley.
LEE: Okay. Well, our place was down on 16th Street, which is down near--very close to it. What was the name of the baseball player that had a restaurant very near where we stayed?
DUNHAM: Oh. Who was a player? Wow.
LEE: Mickey Mantle's place.
DUNHAM: Oh, really? Okay.
LEE: Yeah, he had a restaurant down there.
DUNHAM: Okay. I didn't know that. I know Joe DiMaggio, the DiMaggio family's from the Bay Area, I know.
LEE: Bay Area, yeah, yeah.
DUNHAM: But I didn't know Mickey Mantle had a place.
LEE: We used to go to their restaurant for Italian food. Yeah, wonderful food. Wonderful food, yeah. Fisherman's Wharf. [Question for Robert: Is it possible that the restaurant in question was Lefty O'Doul's Restaurant and Cocktail Bar, on Geary in SF? Mantle probably wasn't old enough or famous enough.]
DUNHAM: So this is '37, you said you were there?
LEE: 1937, that's right. [Actually in Oakland it would be late 1937 and early 1938. I returned to Hawaii in June 1938.--MC]
DUNHAM: Yeah. So you've lived on Hawaii all your life, and I know you've done this tour, but what was California like?
LEE: Well, when I was there, that semester that I had there, I formed some friendships that when I went back years later I was able to find a couple of these old friends. In those days, the one physical thing I remember was I joined a rowing club. We rowed longboats in the harbor, which was very different from our canoes. But I had been a canoe paddler for some time, from the time I was a kid, too. When we didn't surf, we canoe paddled.
DUNHAM: Yeah. So did you try surfing in California, in the frigid ocean of California?
LEE: No, no, it was too cold. I went into the ocean one time, down in the ocean at--oh, what's the popular beach over the--?
DUNHAM: Pacifica?
LEE: Around Pacifica. Yeah, that's what they call--
DUNHAM: Now they have the big competition there. I'm blanking, but yeah.
LEE: Oh, that may be. But in those days, the water was freezing cold.
DUNHAM: Well, no, it still is.
LEE: Yeah. There were no such things as wetsuits in those days. Thank you, thank you, thank you. I'm probably wiggling around too much for you.
DUNHAM: Oh, no, you're fine. Don't worry. I'll keep an eye on it. But stay. And if we need to take a break, let me know. Was University High then predominantly white, or all white?
LEE: Oh, yeah. Yeah, sure. This was before the black people had moved to California, really. I think that most of the Negro population, or what--is it right to call them Negroes?
DUNHAM: Well, Negro to black to African American. Whatever you're--
LEE: Yeah, African American. Well, the African American moving to California was not until during and after the war.
DUNHAM: Yeah, that was the big second wave of migration. There certainly were a fair number in the Oakland Bay Area.
LEE: Oh, yeah. Oh, that's entirely true.
DUNHAM: But probably not in Uni High, I take it.
LEE: Yeah, that's right. Yeah, that's entirely true. I didn't have a big circle of friends, at that time; mostly just classmates.
DUNHAM: And you don't remember any Asians being in the school? Because you might've noticed that, having been with so many Asians growing up, if they were or weren't there, or not.
LEE: Not that I can recall. Not that I really got involved with.
DUNHAM: Did you get over to San Francisco some? Do you remember anything about it?
LEE: Somewhat. Somewhat. In those days, the bridges had just been developed, and the ferries were still running. So it was cheaper to go in the ferry than it was to pay the toll on the bridge.
DUNHAM: What did you do for fun during your time in Oakland and Berkeley?
LEE: Oh, as I say, just kicked around. Did what small kids do.
DUNHAM: Was the plan to stay there indefinitely? Or was it going to be for a short time?
LEE: No. No, no. The plan was that since we were on the mainland and we had spent all of $60 a ticket to get there on the ship--five days of travel for sixty bucks--we thought, "Well, now, while we're here--." Mother kept the car, and we did travel around and visit other relatives and friends. She had family, relatives. Her mother, of course, their family had settled in the Los Angeles area, so we had visited there, as far as, oh, Oceanside, I think, and so forth.
DUNHAM: Oh, well, that's where Candice's parents--that's where Candice is from, actually, north of San Diego. So her parents, from Kauai, met in Southern Cal, and then Candice moved to Oceanside at the age of two.
FUKUMOTO: One.
DUNHAM: One, excuse me. Her parents are still there.
LEE: Oh, you're from Oceanside? There are two highways, beginning from US 1, I guess it is, inland. Very similar numbers. Every time I used to go to visit my friends in Oceanside, I invariably took the wrong street going inland, and had to backtrack.
DUNHAM: Yeah. Well, because the Pacific Coast Highway there, it ends, because the big military, the navy base is there. So you can't get through there; you have to go the main one.
LEE: Go back up to 5, that's right, that's right.
DUNHAM: Water's a little warmer in Oceanside. A little more surfing there. But did you get a chance to--?
LEE: Not only surfing, they've got canoeing there.
DUNHAM: Oh. So did you get a chance to do that when you were there?
LEE: No, I watched. I watched and razzed them. "Hey, don't you know what you're doing?"
DUNHAM: So they weren't quite as skillful as you were then, huh?
LEE: No. But now, believe me, I'd be lost in a canoe nowadays, that's for sure.
DUNHAM: Before we get to Hawaii and where we want to go, anything else about your adventures in California and the mainland, as a teenager?
LEE: No, but I thought it was wonderful to be there. I thought it was a wonderful, wonderful time. I know I got a personal education that people would kill for today. I was living in that lumber camp. It was just a tremendous experience. Lumbering, in those days, was extremely dangerous. And every time we went out--we went out with the lumberers--one of the things they did--in those days, the power was given by steam engines on skips, to move these huge trees. One day I was taken out with my brother and my dad. They were going out and they were going to top some trees. Do you know what it is to top a tree?
FUKUMOTO: I'm not sure. Sounds scary, though.
LEE: They up the tree. They go about 150 or 200 feet, because these are huge, huge, original-growth pine trees. At the top, to fell that tree--they call it felling. These guys would be so expert that they could drop it down just in a certain place here and there, you see, by cutting it off in a certain way. These trees had to be topped. In other words, the top branches had to be cut off, so that when the tree fell, it didn't split, you see, so it remained a log. So a fellow would go up the tree. As he went up the tree, with a belt that went all the way around it--these trees can be as wide as this area here, or wider--he would get up and he would take a leather belt that he was attached to, and flip it, you see. Flip it up and then crawl up, and flip it again, until he got up to the top of the tree. In the process, of course, he encountered branches. But he has a nice sharp axe. He'd chop the branch off, you see? Down comes the branch. He'd get out of the way. Sometimes the branches were bigger than my house. But anyway, but they chop these out.
Now he's up at the top of the tree. He'd been up there since dawn, trying to--now he's up there 150 or more feet up in the air. Now he's going to chop the top of the tree off, so that it falls from there to the ground, right? Not only that, but he has to let that thing fall in such a manner that it doesn't destroy the equipment on the ground, you see? So he's getting extra pay for that. Maybe a whole fifty cents an hour more. But he's up there, and it's truly dangerous. Because if he doesn't chop it right, when that tree breaks off--he's strapped in. If that tree splits, he's killed.
DUNHAM: Oh, wait. Is he coming down with the tree?
LEE: No, no. No, he rides it.
DUNHAM: He's topping it above him.
LEE: He tops it. And that's what I remember, was he topped the tree, and the top came down, and there's sixty or seventy feet of huge tree coming down, right? Then the rest of the tree whips back and forth, and he rides it.
FUKUMOTO: Are you serious?
LEE: Oh, I'm serious. He rides that until it stops whipping, you see? Then he can come back down, and then they can chop the tree down. It's felled in a spot that can be controlled, you see?
DUNHAM: Well, I hate to ask, but did you see any injuries while this was--?
LEE: No, I didn't, fortunately. I'll tell you what I did see. While we were in the lumber camp there, we were fed in the mess hall. Father and the two boys, fed with the other men in the mess hall. I couldn't believe how much food those lumbermen would eat in the morning.
DUNHAM: Well, it's a pretty serious workout.
LEE: Because it was nothing to see them have a full steak.
FUKUMOTO: So that's not a joke.
LEE: No, it's not a joke.
FUKUMOTO: That's real. That's real. Because you see the images of a lumberjack eating, right?
LEE: Yeah. They have fifteen pancakes.
DUNHAM: Well, you can't run out of energy at the top of the tree. [laughs]
LEE: Oh, no, that's right. But that experience, as I say, I can remember that. And I will always remember watching that. As I say, that was that one experience. Now the tree has fallen, right? But it's in the wrong place. So they have a donkey engine, a steam engine on a skip, a large skip, which was made of two full-size trees that it's mounted on top of. And it's moved by its own power. A cable is taken up to another tree, someplace else in the forest, and hooked on. Now the winch on this power thing pulls itself up to where it needs to be. You see? That was another dangerous thing, too, because that cable can snap. And if the cable snaps under all that tension, it just clears everything in front of it. If anyone's standing there, he's eliminated.
DUNHAM: So did your father know a lumberjack, or how did you get this opportunity?
LEE: The lumberjack was a friend of my father's. Actually, his brother, my father's friend's brother. But he was a lumberjack and he was the foreman there, so he opened that place up to us.
DUNHAM: What an experience.
LEE: It was a tremendous experience, because that was all the very first growth. Now everything up there is second growth, you see, because that was from years and years ago. But those huge trees--. Then they would drag them with this power, and the dragging of the tree--it was hooked onto one side and dragged. There was no way of lifting it out then, you see; it had to be dragged to a point where it could then be picked up by a separate winch and put on a truck to be taken to the mill. See? The grooves in the earth there, from where they had been dragging trees through, could be forty and fifty feet deep.
DUNHAM: I know this time in the mainland was a tremendous opportunity, but are you missing Hawaii at all?
