LEE: No, I really wasn't. I really wasn't. Thank you. Of course, my friends that were here were busy riding horses and surfing, doing all those things and so forth. Then when I returned, I got back into that life, too.
DUNHAM: Did you correspond at all when you were there?
LEE: Not much. No, no.
DUNHAM: You were too busy living.
LEE: Just everybody knew I had a couple of close friends here that I kept in touch with, yes. Those friends, one of them was killed in the war and another one moved away. At my high school, when we graduated--I graduated in the year before the war started, so in 1941--I had already been employed with one of the Big Five companies. Castle & Cooke, in this case. My last day of work was December 5, when I went home to spend the weekend, expecting to go back to my job on December 8. But on December 8, I had no home. My home had been taken over by the Marine Corps. Because my home had the only telephone at Aiea, see? So the Marines moved into the house. We evacuated the house, of course, and my parents went to live with my sister in town, so the house was open. There's a telephone there, so the Marine commandant moved right in with his troops, for about four or five days. About three days, I think, three or four. Then my mother went home to reclaim her house, and she had a major cleanup job to do because these guys--it had rained, and their boots and so forth. So poor gal.
DUNHAM: This is where?
LEE: In Aiea. It was on a knoll above the pumping plant. That's why I was able to observe so much in Pearl Harbor. I was actually up high, looking out, you see.
DUNHAM: Yeah. Well, we're near the end of this tape, so maybe I'll ask you a little bit about your year after graduating high school--
LEE: Oh, my lords.
DUNHAM: --and then we'll start with Pearl Harbor right at the beginning of tape three, we promise.
LEE: Okay, all right.
DUNHAM: So tell me about working at Castle & Cooke. How'd you get that job?
LEE: Oh, I got into Castle & Cooke in the usual way. I went down there and say, "I'm not going to go to college, but I would like to learn your business." Because in those days, that was the way that the large companies trained their personnel. We didn't need to go to college. In fact, they preferred we did not, you see. If you went to college that meant that you had other aspirations. The businesses had long since decided that the people who came down, these very well-educated men who came down from the mainland to Hawaii--and there were some every year who came to Hawaii and settled into these businesses and learned the business, and then went home. So then that meant training another adventurer from US mainland. So they got over that. So we local boys, even if we didn't go to the university, as long as we were fairly bright and willing to learn the business the way they wanted it done--at that time, we were very much an island, of course.
DUNHAM: What was their business, and what were you doing for them, to start?
LEE: Well, mostly sugar factories and shippers. I got into the shipping end of the Castle & Cooke Company. They were the representatives for the Matson Navigation Company, the main shipper to Hawaii here. They were the representatives here, and I was to be one of the boys who became expert at loading freighters and keeping the ships going, you see. However, at the day that the war started, December 7, a Sunday, I then, as I say--
DUNHAM: That changed your trajectories and everything.
LEE: It changed everything.
DUNHAM: Well, during that year, were you still able to surf and enjoy the finer things in life?
LEE: Oh, yes. I was a happy young businessman, really. I had a car and I had access to buying clothing at McInerny's store, a very nice store.
DUNHAM: Do you remember your starting wage?
LEE: Yes, I do, as a matter of fact. I had been working earlier, for an industrial company, at fifty dollars a month. So when I went to work for Castle & Cooke, I got a tremendous raise; I went to sixty. But there were a lot of other things, too. There were a lot of, you might say, fringe benefits, of course.
DUNHAM: Like?
LEE: One of them being a happy young bachelor in Honolulu.
DUNHAM: Did you live on your own in Honolulu or were you--?
LEE: No, as a matter of fact, I still lived with my parents for a while. As I say, I was still living with my parents a year later. I joined in January, see, and went through the whole year. Had gone through the office boy branch, had gotten out of the office boy rating, and was actually in a business setting. But the office boys were trained, because you went through the entire company and you became familiar with all these things, you see? Then of course, the company also represented a large number of the plantations locally here, and that was another factoring, they called it. So anyway, I didn't get into the factoring end; I got into the shipping end, which was fine with me.
DUNHAM: What about your other high school friends and friends from before, and of different ethnicities and what have you? What are they doing, what types of things?
LEE: Yeah. Well, I would point out that the Oriental kids--I say Oriental, meaning Asian, usually--were the kids that really took advantage of going to U of H in large numbers, really, because their parents were very, very education-minded. Keep in mind that the Japanese that came to Hawaii on contract were from the very bottom of the Japanese social scale. I think you're probably aware of that. When they came here, they came here to work for a dollar a day. That was fantastic, apparently. But they also had housing, and they had a pretty good life. Also after the contract is over, if they wish, they could stay on here. And many did, of course. Established themselves in small stores. But also got used to the American way. Got used to a different lifestyle, really.
DUNHAM: And the Hawaiian way, too.
LEE: And Hawaii, too. But it was basically among an American society. Very many--let's say a fair number--of my mother's friends who were in the Aiea area, for example, had stores or other forms of occupation, saved up their money, were very, very frugal. Saved up and moved back to Japan, and found that they were not happy there anymore, even in their own old hometowns. So very many of our Japanese people came back to Hawaii.
But you notice I mentioned to you that they had had their kids educated in Japanese schools, as well as American schools. A lot of their kids went to Japan to be educated in the Japanese style, which they considered quite good. Went back to Japan to be educated--I guess because their parents hadn't had that opportunity in Japan, but they still liked the Japanese way of educating. So as the war started, many of those kids were trapped in Japan, as you're aware of.
DUNHAM: Well, we're near the end of this tape. Anything else kind of pre-Pearl Harbor, before we switch tapes?
LEE: Oh, yeah. Pre-Pearl Harbor? As I say, my life in Hawaii was quite good.
DUNHAM: Your father is still working in the civil service?
LEE: My father's still working. Yeah. By that time, he has moved into the position of being very much in charge of the water pumping station. Even though he has only one hand, he is also the chief mechanic. There were three water pumps in the water station. Two of them always available to run, and usually ran. The third one was a standby, or under repair, see? The responsibility was really quite important, keeping the water station going. But I lived there, and so as a small child, my father was able to teach me my numbers. It was wonderful to have my parents right there.
DUNHAM: Yeah. And your mother's been raising the four children?
LEE: My mother's been raising the four children, more or less as a housewife. She took side jobs. At one time, she was the postmaster there. As I say, she had many friendships, too.
DUNHAM: Yeah. Both her parents are still living?
LEE: They're still living. Okay? My grandfather has a stroke in 1939 or '40, and with my grandmother, they then move to Aiea with us. So as the war starts, my grandfather and grandmother are living in a bedroom overlooking Pearl Harbor, see? So as Pearl Harbor occurs, we have to vacate the house, because our house is up on a knoll.
DUNHAM: Well, let's pause here, and then we'll start on December 6, if that's okay.
DUNHAM: Okay, it's tape three, July 26, 2014, with Robert Lee.
LEE: Three tapes, wow!
DUNHAM: Yeah. We're almost to December 7, 1941, but Robert just remembered something else very interesting, talking about when you did some work in the fields, right? No child labor laws.
LEE: Yeah. As I say, no child labor laws. So Brother took a job with a friend of my father's, [who] was the manager for the California Packing Corporation. Their pineapple fields needed help, so Brother, who went out to work in the pineapple fields, said, "Hey, there's room for Robert, too." So I went out in the summertime and lived in the barracks in the pineapple fields, and went out before dawn every morning, at six o'clock in the morning. Well, it was summertime. Let's say it was already dawn when I got on the trucks, and we were taken out to the fields with the other young boys, my age or a bit older. But we were smaller, so we were not in the position of harvesting the pineapples. That was done by the men, who then put a large bag on their shoulders, put a bunch of picked pineapples in it, carried it back to the end of these rows--which you still see in the fields--dumped them there. Then we kids, with a very sharp knife and a very thick glove on our left hand, cut the tops off the pineapples, and then put these pineapples into redwood boxes. Then the boxes were picked up, thrown onto a truck, and taken out to where they could be taken to the cannery. But my job in the field was, right at dawn, to get out in the morning, go out and do that.
At noon, the Korean ladies in the camp prepared a lunch for us, in what we called our little ukana boxes, of rice, mostly, and vegetables, and a little bit of meat and so forth. That was our lunch. We had thirty minutes to get that done, and then we went right back to work. We went back to work, and then at six in the evening, the truck came by again. It was a twelve-hour day. Came back then and picked us up and took us back to the camp, where we had a fast bath, and then relaxed for the rest of the evening, until maybe seven or eight o'clock, when we went to bed.
DUNHAM: Yeah, exhausted.
LEE: Got our dinner. This is where I really learned to care for Oriental food--for Chinese food and Japanese food and Korean food. But Korean food, a little hard for youngsters to take. Their love their spices. No, but we got used to it. That's where I learned how to eat with chopsticks. If you didn't eat with chopsticks, you didn't eat. To this day, when I have my rice, I'll bring the plate up to my mouth and stuff it in. All my friends look at that and say, "What are you doing?" I say, "I'm eating."
DUNHAM: Not starving.
LEE: No. Anyway.
DUNHAM: So did you do that the whole summer?
LEE: I did it for the summer. Now, there was a little store there, and we could go, and for five cents, we could get a candy bar, which was a real treat. But that was like paying the company store for all this stuff. Then of course, we had to pay the ladies that were making our lunches, too.
DUNHAM: So what are you making?
LEE: So at the end of the first summer there, I netted twelve dollars.
FUKUMOTO: Nice.
DUNHAM: That sounds about right, yeah.
LEE: But the heck is, in those days, twelve dollars was pretty good. We took it back and it took me through most of my high school that I went back to. Because I think after that--
DUNHAM: But you didn't go back there another summer?
LEE: Oh, yes, I did. I went back for a second summer. In the second summer that I went there, I was a bit older, but I was still not big enough to harvest the fields. I have a funny story about the harvesters, too, by the way. The harvesters were the men who got off the truck and went into the field. But as soon as they got off the truck, each one would rush over to the pineapple field and pick the largest, ripest, prettiest pineapple they could find, break it off, cut the top off, and reach inside with their sharp knives, and make a soup out of the inside. Then they would put that same thing back on its own same plant; then they would go off and do the harvesting. At the end of the day, that pineapple had sat in the hot sun all day long, you see. So at six p.m., they come, and each of those men would take his pineapple, jump back on the truck, and drink his alcohol all the way back to the camp. Because it had been fermenting all day long.
DUNHAM: They fermented their own--
LEE: This was their booze. They called it pineapple swipes. Have you ever heard of pineapple swipes?
DUNHAM: No. We're learning a lot.
LEE: Well, that's right. But anyway.
DUNHAM: What was the ethnicity of the--?
LEE: Filipinos, mostly. The bosses were Korean men.
DUNHAM: Six days a week, you're working?
LEE: Yeah.
DUNHAM: What do you do on your day off? Do you get to come home or--?
LEE: Now, wait a minute. Wait a minute. I forget whether it was five days--I think we only did five days, because I know my mother would come and--
DUNHAM: You got to come home for the weekends?
LEE: Took us home for the weekends, yeah. That's when we changed our clothes and got baths, genuine baths. Then we went back. But that was the year that President Roosevelt and Congress declared Social Security. My Social Security number ended in 822. As far as I can tell, I am the 822nd person in Hawaii to get a Social Security number. Very low number, as far as I understand. But I got it the very first year that Social Security came out.
DUNHAM: Since you brought that up, what kind of impacts did Social Security, did the New Deal have in Hawaii?
LEE: Fantastic! Fantastic! Because out in the fields there, not only did we get Social Security, we got a minimum wage. A minimum wage. My minimum wage went to twenty-seven-and-a-half cents an hour, and I was ecstatic.
DUNHAM: Do you know what it was before?
LEE: I have no idea.
DUNHAM: I know you said you saved twelve dollars that first summer, but it wasn't very much. It wasn't twenty-seven-and-a-half.
LEE: It wasn't very much for a twelve-hour day, but then of course in addition to that I'm pretty sure that they had to provide some health benefit, and also I believe there was a meal benefit there, too, because the following year--
DUNHAM: Without having to pay for it. Because you got meals, but you had to pay.
LEE: Without having to pay for it. Yeah, that's right. It was part of the pay. But I think my take-home pay, the part that was my pay, I know went to--I say twenty-seven-and-a-half cents an hour, because I remember that figure so well. I don't know how long that was. I know that when I moved to the cannery--because the following year, I moved to the cannery in the city. Then my pay was in the thirty cents an hour rate. In addition to that, we could go to the cafeteria, CPC's cafeteria, and have a meal, a basic meal, all of the bread and the pineapple jam that we could possibly consume, and a cup of coffee. You see? So that was our lunch hour. Or it was still our lunch half hour.
DUNHAM: Yeah, yeah, half hour, yeah.
LEE: But we didn't have the twelve-hour day. I know we had an eight-hour day.
DUNHAM: Did you have friends, when you were a kid, who had to work there during the school year, or instead of going to school, work, that you know of?
LEE: Well, I had friends that worked after school, yeah.
DUNHAM: In the fields?
LEE: In my case, I lived in the country. I couldn't do that; I had to go home. But it also kept me out of most of the sports. Which was okay with me. I still have my knees. A lot of my friends played football, and coaching in those days was rudimentary, to say the least.
DUNHAM: Yeah. So maybe you told us before, but what was your commute to school?
LEE: Oh. Well, it started with the train, from the railroad station right there in front of us, when I was a small kid. We went on the train to Kalihi Street, in Kalihi. There was a bus there that took us from the Kalihi Street railroad station up to Kapalama School. My mother, by then--no, we went home. For the first couple years, we went home on the train, too. I don't really remember much about that. My sister was in charge then. She's five years older than I was, so she saw to it that I behaved. Also my father had all of his friends on the railroads, and he knew all the engineers and knew all the conductors. So we couldn't get in any trouble, as a small child, or the conductors would take me on the train and I'd go ride around with them, too. Anyway, then my mother got a car, eventually, so she would pick us up in the afternoon. But we would go to town on the train, while Mother did other things. Then she would just drive into town from Fort Shafter back to Aiea, in those days, by the road that existed then. It took us thirty minutes to get from the city to the Aiea town, which now is done in possibly five or six minutes.
DUNHAM: Well, I guess take us to December. You go to sleep December 6, you've been working in Castle & Cooke about a year.
LEE: Okay. Anyway, December 6, I'm working about a year, and I'm living the life of a happy young bachelor, twenty-year-old boy.
DUNHAM: Things are good in Hawaii.
LEE: Yes, they were. So then all of a sudden one day, I wake up and the war is--basically, the explosions woke me. I was sleeping. I had been out on a date the night before, and I had driven around the end, past Makapu'u, with my used car, with a couple, two or three friends. Then I come back over Nu'uanu Pali, back to visit with a friend, at one o'clock in the morning, and then I went home. So I didn't get home until approximately 1:30, two o'clock in the morning. Sleeping in, of course. Mother was very understanding.
Here, my memory escapes me a little bit. For some reason or other, I had used my mother's car the day before, and I had her car keys in my pocket. At the time that the war started, with all this explosion, the very first thing happened, that my friend, Lester {Irish?}--who was spending the night at my house, too, with me--he and I rushed into my grandfather's room, where he was bedridden. He had had a stroke; he was being taken care of by my grandmother. The two of them were living in this small bedroom, adjacent to the bathroom. The flies here, are they bothering you at all?
DUNHAM: I'm fine. Are you doing okay?
LEE: I just had to brush away a fly; I just didn't know whether you were really bothered.
DUNHAM: No, I'm okay.
LEE: Okay. Anyway, my friend Lester and myself picked up my grandfather, on his mattress, carried him down the hill, to where the car was parked, about a hundred yards from the house; put him in the back of my open car, and drove him to a cave up in the hill that we knew about, about-- oh, beyond, beyond the school. My father had been practicing--well, he had actually been growing mushrooms, my father, very industriously growing mushrooms. He wanted to establish a mushroom growing business, so he had this cave, growing them in the dark. But we rushed my grandfather and grandmother. And a couple of ladies from the nearby--one of them, the railroad station manager's wife, I think; and another was a lady, a friend of my mother's, who had a small shop near the area. Then we took them up and took them to this cave and expected the rest of the family to come up in Mother's car. But they didn't come and didn't come. In the meantime, more of the attack is going on, and I'm watching the attack still going on, from up on the hill there. I thought, gee, it's strange they're not coming. Then I felt in my pocket and realized I had my mother's car keys; then jumped in my car with my friend, and we rushed back down into Aiea, to the town.
Just then, a Japanese aircraft came through that area right there, in the little corner of the harbor, shooting his gun just sort of indiscriminately. But it caused everybody to rush off and jump into the weeds nearby. But he didn't hit anything or anybody, and he went on to do other things. I'm not even sure why he thought he was needed to fire there at all; but anyway, he saw people and maybe he saw them. So then I rushed back in. Then we decided, well, heck, we'll just see what else is going on.
About that time, the first boats that had been servicing the anchored ships that knew that the pier was there started coming in with a lot of the young sailors, who had jumped into the very oil-coated waters. Very heavy fuel oil had spilled into the water, from the penetrated ships. The Oklahoma, for example, had already turned over. Because before I'd even taken my grandfather and grandmother up to the cave, I had watched the Oklahoma turn right over. That was the first part of it; when I was still looking out my bedroom window, that still was happening. Then even when I was still up there looking, at that same early time, the Arizona exploded in this huge ball of fire. I can still remember seeing this huge ball of fire come up. I say a ball of fire; actually, it was a number of tongues of fire that shot up separately, with a very loud explosion, and a very strong wave of pressure came through. Then the hissing of that fire remains in my memory to this day, the enormous hissing. Now, that fire was more than a mile away. The ship that exploded was more than a mile away. But the hull of the ship turned as red as that [points]. No, if you've ever seen a welder working a piece of metal, you know what I mean? A blacksmith, perhaps. The whole ship's hull turned that color, from the inside, before the explosion, you see? That whole hull turned that color from the inside burning of the explosion there. Remember that the armament on that ship, the thickness of the hull there, was fourteen inches thick, but it still turned the entire hull that I was looking at that same color. Of course, that destruction, of course, killed those fellows that were there. They were just done right then. Then the ship exploded, with that tremendous roar and that great fire. Now, the fire really was tongues of fire hundreds of feet up in the air. And the very enormous hissing, I can remember.
FUKUMOTO: What were you thinking? What were you feeling, as you're witnessing this?
LEE: "My gosh, look at that." Except of course, oh, my God. The thing is, when I looked across there and I knew, I said, "There's no way--." I knew it then, that there was no way that the fellows that were in the middle of that could've survived. I realized that. Then as I say, that part happened; then I took my grandfather and grandmother up to this cave. Came back to the pier, to the pier area.
DUNHAM: Can I ask, going up to the cave, had that been an idea that had been in place in case of--?
LEE: No. No, it had not. I just wanted to get up there, and I expected the rest of the family to come there, too, with the exception of Dad, who was busy pumping water. He had a couple of assistants that were there, too, by the way. Mr. Schordt was there, and I forget who the other person was.
DUNHAM: Was he already there, or did he rush down there?
LEE: Well, he rushed down there, because he had been sleeping, too. There was a twenty-four watch there. Incidentally, at that time the Marines also were guarding the area, too. I have a picture of one of the Marines that became a very close friend of mine, in the house there. I'm trying to find his family, to give the picture to them.
DUNHAM: Yeah. Well, we'd love to see it.
LEE: Anyway, so that happened.
DUNHAM: So you'd gotten back to the house.
LEE: So then I came back to the house, and we had that little experience of this Japanese plane coming through. It was a dive bomber, had fixed wheels and a low wing. He sort of buzzed through. But he gave us all a good scare, that's for sure.
But then about that same time, these boats that had rushed out into the harbor and started pulling these fellows aboard, those that had [been] injured aboard started lining up against the Solace. The Solace was a hospital ship. It was anchored much closer to us, just across the harbor. These small boats started pulling into the Solace, with the injured and the burned there. We could look across and quite clearly see that going on, pulling these people aboard. A lot of those actually were bodies, by then. I found out later that the Solace had called for a barge to be put up on the far side, and many of the bodies were just put over to the barge, so they say. I don't know; I didn't see that, but that's what I understand. But anyway, then we looked across; we saw that. But meanwhile, these other boats are coming into the pier there, where we are at, bringing these fellows who were just covered with this heavy oil.
So immediately, we knew what to do. We hooked up several hoses to the water supply there, and we started washing these fellows down. My mother came down with several cakes of what they called Fels-Naptha Soap. The naphtha soap cuts grease. My father had used it for years, because his was a mechanicing type of job. Mother used it to both clean the person, and also to wash the clothing, you see? So we washed these fellows down with this soap. We even had them wash themselves down, too, believe me. But it was quite busy and we had, oh, I'd say four hoses running at the same time. We'd pull them off the pier. We took them into the area between the railroad station and the yard there, just ran the hoses and washed these fellows. As they washed off, these guys, the boats kept coming in with more fellows. The boys that had been washed off jumped on the boats and went back looking for their ships. All the ships that had been anchored in the harbor, pretty well, most of them seemed to have cut loose and were cruising in the harbor. Not very many were going out. I don't know if any went out; some must've, of course, through the channel. But when it came to the battleship Nevada--which I don't really remember seeing move, but of course, it did--it was the battleship that was anchored closest to us, within a mile away, but on the very tip of Ford Island. It cut loose and went down the harbor. But as it did, it was attacked heavily and was sinking, before it got to the harbor exit. So they beached it in the low water right there, and let it sink right there.
I do remember seeing the dive bombers coming in on the ships in the harbor. For example, the service ship Vestal had been tied up beside the Arizona. All of those ships were tied up in tandem on Battleship Row. There was a battleship inside and outside, you see? That was the lack of piers. On the far side, ordinarily, the aircraft carriers would've been parked there; they were out. But their piers were occupied singly, by cruisers, and in one case, the old battleship, the Utah. Apparently, the Japanese expected the carriers to be in. As you know, they studied Pearl Harbor for a long time. So they decided that that would be the first ships to be attacked. Sister, who woke up in the morning quite early with a friend of hers who also stayed with her overnight, the two of them went down to the pier to make eyes at the sailors, I think. They were cute girls anyway. The sailors that were coming away from the time--it was that early in the morning. Anyway, they're watching the ship. Somehow--I'm still not clear on this, as to how they could see over to the other side of Ford Island, but Ford Island was then under attack, where the battleship Utah was being torpedoed. These huge columns of water were rising up into the air. They thought, "Boy, that's really something." These two girls are talking: "Gee, look at that." All of a sudden, the Utah turned right over. Just turned right over. After they got over their surprise, they rushed back to tell my dad, wake my dad up--actually; by that time, he was awake, too--to tell him what had happened. Then the other ships that were there also took heavy damage from torpedoes and so forth. Out in the harbor, many of those ships that were moving out came under attack from the Japanese dive bombers, see? I guess that's when most of them decided they would cut loose and keep moving.
The hospital ship Solace remained in position, remained spotted right there, a white ship with big red crosses on it. And as I say, had these small boats lining up to bring the injured over to be serviced right there in this hospital. My mother tells the story, when she went back and reclaimed her home after being out of it for three or four days, the hospital ship Solace is still spotted right there. One of the Japanese midget submarines that got into the harbor--we think there were three--after it released it torpedoes, probably at the Utah, then went over and parked underneath the Solace, submerged underneath the Solace. Every night, in the very early morning hours, it would come up enough so that it could replace its air, you see, and then sink back down. But after the third night, it was detected. So when it came up, it was detected, and when it went back down--Mother is at home, in the house--and all of a sudden there's a great roar and a "Whoo" and a big amount of activity out in the harbor. The Solace cut its lines and raced away, and in came a destroyer, depth bombing what they knew to be a Japanese midget submarine. They did, they destroyed it right there, in forty feet of water. Right there. It killed the crew, of course. But apparently, it started up and ran away a little way, but it was then detected and pulled out of the water; the Navy pulled it out of the water. It was washboarded, really, from one end to the other, by these explosions, and taken over and displayed in front of the submarine base. They put it on cement pylons, over in front of the submarine base, as a trophy. But it was this midget submarine. It was one of the five.
One of the others, of course, was beached, lost at Kaneohe. You've seen those pictures, I'm sure. Then they think the others, one of them was destroyed trying to come into the harbor. We know about that one. Then the other two were thought to have come into the harbor, have done their torpedoing, and then probably, they think, taken over into the West Loch of Pearl Harbor, which as you know, is a separate loch, and sunk there or beached there. Probably were underwater when they had the big 1944 explosions there. I'm sure you're aware of the ammunition destruction there.
DUNHAM: At West Loch, yeah.
LEE: And the West Loch destruction where seven or eight of the LSTs were destroyed and very many lives lost again there, too. But they think that in the cleanup of taking all of the debris, from these exploded ships there, that they also picked up the hulls of the two submarines that they think were there.
DUNHAM: I had not heard that. Wow.
LEE: And took them out and dropped them in the ocean, a mile or so off of Pearl Harbor, where they are today.
DUNHAM: Wow. Well, I want to go back to your personal experience, when you and your mom were helping wash these wounded and oiled--
LEE: I'm sorry. Yes, go ahead.
DUNHAM: No, no, that's okay. Was that walking distance from your home, or did you--?
LEE: Oh, yeah. We walked. We walked right there. Yeah, walked right there. So there's a lot of foot traffic right there. A lot of fellows came in, washed off, and went back. The thing is that that's when the activity really started firing back. Because it was during that second attack, you see, that the very few aircraft that got off the ground from the Haleiwa airfield came over the eastern end of Ford Island at about 150 feet, just right off the water, you see? Came through in a V formation. Five brown fighter aircraft came through, probably to let our American troops know that there were aircraft. Every ship on the harbor started shooting at them. The air was just filled with antiaircraft explosions. Of course, these kids, these aircraft P-40 fighters. Eventually, they reformed. Paul has a picture of one of those. It's actually a painting; it's not a photo, by any means. All the photos you see of Pearl Harbor under attack are taken by the Japanese. You know that, I'm sure. But anyway, this picture is of a Japanese aircraft being attacked by an American P-40. Paul has a picture of that, and he has the signatures of the five aircraft pilots, on this picture. Those five aircraft pilots then signed Paul's picture. So anyway, I saw that part of it.
DUNHAM: What happens with you and your mother? How long are you doing the hosing and--?
LEE: Okay. Finally, as I say, the washing continued. But at about that same time, the father of a very close friend of mine, Mr. {Seger?}, was an employee of McKesson, Robbins. Had been a Navy corpsman, and was driving an auxiliary ambulance made from a McKesson and Robbins delivery van. You see, before the war, we expected trouble with the Japanese, so we had a civilian defense organization. We had a blood bank, we had storage, and we had sirens, and we had a doctors group made up, you see. Then also we had these ambulances that had been fixed with, you might say, auxiliary stretcher holders. Four stretchers in one of those. My friend had a young man, a young Oriental guy, one of the company people driving with him. Went to Hickam Field, and they started picking up bodies off of the airfield of the people who'd been over there. This young fellow, nineteen or twenty. He stood it as long as he could, and he just ran away. Poor guy. But they were picking up bodies, taking them over to the gathering places and so forth. So my friend then said, "Well--." He'd lost his helper, the other half of his stretcher, really. So he came over to Aiea looking for me, and I was there. He said, "Come on with me. I need help." So I then went with him, back to Hickam. By then, we didn't have to pick up any of the dead. They had been pretty well picked up. But we had a lot of the people who had been wounded; we moved some of them around. Mostly by then, also we moved a lot of people from various helping stations to the infirmaries. Did quite a bit of that. Did some work in that respect. We're now getting into the afternoon, you see. It was decided that all of the personnel, all the dependents living on Hickam Field, needed to be moved away from the anticipated attack. Everyone, of course, we all expected to be invaded. There was no question about it.
DUNHAM: Had you heard of dependents leaving the area before? I interviewed one gentleman whose father worked at the Navy base, and whose family left in November, and indicated that other family had evacuated in November.
LEE: In November? Perhaps.
DUNHAM: Or so, yeah.
LEE: But I don't know of any particular case. I think most of that was just on a normal exchange.
DUNHAM: It may have been. He was young at the time, so it may have been. So that afternoon, though, by afternoon they're evacuating all the dependents off the base?
LEE: They're evacuating all the dependents from the bases, you see? In our case, we're over at Hickam Field. We have half a dozen ambulances, I guess. So we started taking the families, the dependent families, from Hickam Field--the service families--up to Kamehameha School, up on the {Kapālama Heights. You see? But this is the afternoon, and it's becoming dark.
DUNHAM: Yeah, it's December.
LEE: By now, we have martial law, and it has been declared that everyone needs to be home by curfew time, six p.m.
DUNHAM: How is that communicated?
LEE: Mostly by radio. By radio. That's how the kids got to the armory the night before. In other words, we had two radio stations, and they were broadcasting pretty much on that. Anyway, so we then load up our ambulance with very many, really, dependents. We had this convoy that then went from Hickam Field up to Kamehameha School, where these people were then to be quartered for the next few days, while this supposed attacked took place. At least there was beds and places to feed them and so forth, and Kamehameha School opened itself up for that. In the process, of course, it's completely dark. So there was an MP [military police] leading us up there. If you had known the road at the time, it was kind of devious, kind of winding. The MP got lost.
DUNHAM: Oh! And the whole convoy.
LEE: So we're the first ambulance in line. And it's completely dark. Okay, it's completely dark. But anyway, my friend Mr. {Seger?} had been up and down the hill a number of times, knew the area very well. Lived nearby. So he led the convoy to the top, by flashing flashlights and just maybe calling and so forth. We led this convoy up to Kamehameha School, and they got up there safely. Okay. All of a sudden, along comes the MP, screaming. Just cursing. He used words I'd never heard before, believe me. "What in the hell do you think you're doing?," and so forth. Really bawled out my friend, who had led the convoy properly.
DUNHAM: Bawled him out for taking the lead, when he got lost?
LEE: Oh, absolutely. It was just amazing.
DUNHAM: Because he was embarrassed and defensive?
LEE: Yeah, defensive. Very defensive. But threatening, really. "Going to kill you."
DUNHAM: Oh, my. He knew you'd finally gotten to the right place, right?
LEE: We got to the right place. But anyway, this all got settled eventually. I think the people that were there, and finally a couple of officers--or officers' wives, anyway--said, "Hey, shut up and go away." Believe me, an officer's wife was very important, believe me. Anyway, so that got settled, but it was kind of an interesting time. So now we're up at the--. Mr. {Seger?} said, "We have to get the ambulance back to the Honolulu Armory, because there are other duties that we have to do." Completely dark. No lights at all, no automobile lights, no house lights. Lots of flame and brightness coming from Pearl Harbor, believe me; everything's burning out there. But no other lights allowed. My friends that were living in Wahiawa had experiences where people did turn on lights, and they were shot out, literally, by patrols from up there at Wheeler Field and Schofield and so forth. A friend of mine became a warden up there. He went around telling people to shut lights [off]. That was part of his experience, too. He eventually became an Army pilot.
Anyway, so now we're taking the ambulance back to the armory, in the dark. So I took off my shirt and I had a white t-shirt on. I walked in front of the ambulance, from Kamehameha School, down the streets, three feet in front of the ambulance. My driver could see in the dark. Then as I came to a break in the trees, I could tell if it's a break in the trees, that's a street. So I would listen. In those days, the cars were noisy, Model-A Fords and Chevrolets and so forth. I would listen. If I heard no cars, I would scoot across the street, with him right behind me. We did that from street to street to street, all the way back to--.
FUKUMOTO: How long did that take you?
LEE: A couple of hours, I guess. Yeah, because I got back to the armory; the armory was exactly where the state capitol is now. That was the Honolulu Armory, on Miller Street right there. But we got back there.
DUNHAM: Are the other ambulances returning with you?
LEE: I have no idea. Their problem was their problem.
DUNHAM: That was enough of a challenge.
LEE: That's right. Anyway, but we got back there.
DUNHAM: This is after getting in at one-thirty the night before and waking up pretty early.
LEE: Yeah. Yeah, well--
DUNHAM: But I'm sure you had a lot of adrenaline.
LEE: We did. Adrenaline we had. Believe me, we had it. Of course, we had a lot of military rations that kept us going, too. I remember that part, too.
DUNHAM: So after you got to the armory, then--?
LEE: Then I got to the armory and walked into the armory. We parked the ambulance on the outside, walked into the brightest, noisiest, most impossible imagination that you can have. All the boys from the ROTC at the University of Hawaii had been told by radio, by hook or crook, "Get down to the Honolulu Armory; you are now in the Army." As I mentioned earlier, here come these kids, as I walked in the door and came through the blackout curtains. A huge curtain had been put across the door and all the windows had been painted out by then. They walked into this brightly lighted room. They were handed a rifle and live ammunition and told to practice with it. And that's what they were doing. There were hundreds of these kids, hundreds of these local boys, in their uniforms.
DUNHAM: Now, did this include--because there probably had been lots of Japanese ROTC, at this point?
LEE: All of them.
DUNHAM: So these are a lot of Japanese?
LEE: All of them. All of them. Absolutely all of them. Ted Tsukiyama was a good example. Very many other, too, friends. The Kuroda brothers, who were at the university, they had come in from Aiea. Kuroda brothers were good friends. And others who had been university students.
DUNHAM: Many of these, you said, have never touched a gun before?
LEE: Sure, they'd never touched a gun.
DUNHAM: So it was a little chaotic.
LEE: Yeah. Well, I had a 22 target rifle, so I knew. My 22 target rifle, of course--did I mention that during the day, I shot at Japanese aircraft with my 22 target rifle.
DUNHAM: I don't think you did mention that.
FUKUMOTO: No, you missed that!
LEE: I'm sorry.
DUNHAM: This was a busy day.
FUKUMOTO: Yeah, there's a lot going on.
LEE: I still have the target rifle; I just gave it to my son-in-law. Yes, I have this 22 target rifle. As the Japanese aircraft are going nearby, diving on, for example, the Vestal--they dove on the Vestal, bombed the Vestal, and the Vestal then came up to the harbor, beached itself in front of our house, not far away. Another Japanese dive bomber dived on it. But then the Vestal shot its wing off and it corkscrewed, like a great corkscrew, right through the air, and crashed in the cane fields, killing the pilot, of course. But that became a souvenir for half the souvenir hunters in Hawaii, believe me. But anyway, I'm sorry I didn't mention that. Yeah.
DUNHAM: So this is from your home?
LEE: From my home, I saw that, yeah. I went back into the house--and this is even before we started watching the sailors, I'm sorry. I went back, took the rifle--I had plenty of ammunition--and I wind away at it for a while. Couldn't hit anything. At least I didn't shoot anything down, I'm sure, with my 22 target rifle. Okay. I hand it over to my sister, who is four or five years younger than me, three or four years younger than me. She shot it for a while, turned it over to her girlfriend, who shot it for a while. So my 22 target rifle really fired a lot of shots at that existing airplane. That was the thing. When this attack was going on, we didn't have to look up to see the aircraft. As they dropped their loads, they then started forming up right over our heads, and we could look directly out, straight ahead of us, and we could see the heads of the pilots in the cockpits and so forth. That was part of that experience. I'm sorry I didn't bring it up before. But that was what they did.
The first group formed up right over our heads, and went back to their ships. They were to be reformed. They were to be rearmed and refueled, and to come back and destroy our fuel supply and our docks and piers. But because they didn't have a clear shot at the aircraft carriers, and they didn't know they were out--the Japanese, of course, were under complete radio silence. They would not receive or send any messages. They would not even receive messages, you see? So they didn't know; no one was able to tell them, no, the carriers are not in the harbor. So that was why their first attack was against the air, where the Utah was sunk. That and the airfields, of course. So without those aircraft carriers being in, the Japanese then had to abbreviate their attack. They had not to carry out the third attack, which would've destroyed the facilities. It was because of that that we were then able to repair our ships in Pearl Harbor.
I tell everybody that I'm talking to that that is the true story of Pearl Harbor. I mean, the attacks, fine; but the true story of Pearl Harbor is the recovery of our fleet. All those ships, with the exception of the Arizona, the Oklahoma and the Utah, all the rest of them were put back in service. Even the destroyer, which had its bow blown off. They put a jury bow on board, took it back to Mare Island, and put a new half of a ship right back on it. Same ship. Of course, the Nevada, as you know, was repaired, refueled, and it took part in the D-Day invasion in Europe in 1944. Same ship.
FUKUMOTO: So is the whole community, were you guys all coming together and just trying to help out?
LEE: That's right. Exactly right. We had our people here. Have you ever heard of the WARDS [Women's Air Raid Defense]?
FUKUMOTO: I think so.
LEE: The WARDS, for the girls, where the girls--this would come to be a very social thing. Had all these girls dressed up in blue uniforms. They were taken up--you've seen pictures of the British and their control of the air from watching the radar screens and all those people? We had such a unit in Hawaii; they were the WARDS, you see. They, of course, were the ones that were there, and they had regular duties with the radar station.
DUNHAM: So air wardens.
LEE: Air wardens, right.
DUNHAM: Volunteer air wardens.
LEE: Yeah, but they called them WARDs. It got to be a very social thing; they had clubs and parties.
DUNHAM: Well, I want to get to that. We want to talk about the Home Front scene there after, but I'm still at December 7. I know there's something you told us off camera that you haven't gotten to. When all these ROTC kids are armed, it was a little frightening right? Because they're practicing--
LEE: They're practicing. Well, it was, to us, very practical.
DUNHAM: It was frightening all around, but they became frightening.
LEE: Yeah, yeah. As it happened, we went to sleep in the ambulance, Fred and myself. We went to sleep on a couple of the stretchers. But they soon came and woke us up.
DUNHAM: In part, that was to barricade yourself from the shooting--is that right--of the--?
LEE: Not being shot.
DUNHAM: Yeah, by the ROTC. Okay, I just wanted to make sure we had that.
LEE: Yeah, we thought that was really--I really don't know. Anyway, they were forming this up, then, to be the Hawaii Territorial Guard. I had taken a rifle but I was not yet in the service, you see? Now, they came and woke us up and we walked around in the dark again. We went down near the piers, museum [chuckles] closer to the armory. There were a number of storage rooms, storage sheds, storage warehouses, with a lot of supplies in them. So we used our ambulance, went back and forth to the armory a couple of times, bringing supplies back to the armory, again walking in the dark.
DUNHAM: Yeah. So you're out in front of the ambulance?
LEE: Out in front of the ambulance, that's right .
DUNHAM: You're doing your guide routine.
LEE: We got pretty practiced at that; it worked out pretty good. Anyway, we did that. Then finally, in the morning, Fred received a message that the ambulance, or his delivery van, was needed back at McKesson Robbins to do a lot of their deliveries from their supplies. So I said, "Okay, that's fine. But hey, Fred, I've got to get back to Aiea; I need clothing and to get cleaned up and so forth." So Fred said, "Yeah, okay, I'll drive you out there." We drove out to the Aiea pumping station. I was going up to the house to get some clothing, steal it back from the Marines, mind you. No, but they were just fine; they were great guys. But they were, they were in the house and they were using our telephone and so forth.
DUNHAM: Had they seen your mom or dad or gotten permission? Or it had just been sort of--?
LEE: No, well, my dad was right there. Yeah, actually, he was living on Marine food by then, sure, and he had access to the house, sure. But the thing is, [I] went back. The station agent came out just then. Mr. {Seger?} and I were there. And he himself, Dad, two or three of the fellows that were working with him, and my friend, who was also there--Lester {Irish?} is the name--the station agent said, "President Roosevelt is going to give a speech to Congress, on the shortwave radio." Shortwave radio was how we received our calls from the mainland. "I'm hooked up to it in my quarters on the second floor of the railroad station. Come up and listen." So we all trooped up and went up to his house and listened on his radio. His second floor looked right straight out over all of Pearl Harbor, second floor windows. While President Roosevelt gave his "Day of Infamy" speech, we're all looking out the windows and watching our ships burn. I remember that. But I remember in one place, in the "day of infamy speech," one place he said, "Very many lives have been lost." He said that; that's part of his speech. We looked at each other and nodded. Because were just beginning then, really, to realize what it was all about. By now, of course, we'd all been exposed to the bodies, some of them. So yeah. I would say that it was at that point, as far as I'm concerned, and my friends, when we really looked at each other and said, "Hey, this is serious."
DUNHAM: Yeah, began to sink in.
LEE: We're really in it. Up to that time, I think it had just been energy that kept us going. Because were on the go. Go, go, go. So anyway, that's basically what it was, as far as that was concerned. I then, soon, went back into town. I forget whether Fred took me--I think he took me back in the ambulance. I said, "Let's go back to the armory. I want to at least be there. No sense in staying at home." I didn't have a house, really. Mother and everybody else was still living in town. I said, "Take me back to the armory. At least the folks know I'm there." So I went back. Then at that time, I said, "Well, I guess I will join the Hawaii Territorial Guard, as well." Meanwhile, all these young fellows who had been handed rifles were put on railroad cars full of ammunition, had been taken by the railroad out to all these spots all over the island, everywhere the railroad went, and put on railroad sidings and forgotten.
DUNHAM: What do you mean?
FUKUMOTO: You mean they forgot--?
LEE: You ask Ted. He said, "We were starving." Because they had forgotten they were out there.
DUNHAM: There's a lot going on.
LEE: Fortunately, there were other military people around us, so finally, these guys went out to these military places in uniforms, said, "Hey, we're starving. We need food." So they got fed. But you ask Ted about that. He can tell you. Yeah, boy.
But anyway, now, okay, that's fine. So here we are in town. Now, I'm in Hawaii Territorial Guard. My first night of guarding, I guarded a school, with my rifle, in the Kakaako area, where they're building all those highrises now. That was a residential area. So I guarded a school there.
DUNHAM: So what kind of training or instruction have you gotten, to this point?
LEE: My former ROTC high school training. But at least I knew what a gun was.
DUNHAM: So it was just, "Go out and guard the school"?
LEE: "Go out and guard," yeah. "You're the guard." Oh, I had a gas mask. I've got my gas mask here; I'll show you when we get off this. Thank you. I've got my gas mask still. And I had this gun and so forth.
DUNHAM: Is the school vacant?
LEE: Vacant, yeah, because the kids were not--everybody stayed home, see? We're still expecting to be invaded, you understand. Because it was still invasion, as far as we were concerned. But in the meantime, I'm called back to the armory, because they realize I do know a little bit about military. So I'm called back to the armory, and I don't know who was guarding Pohukaina Elementary School after that, if anybody. But anyway, I don't think the Japanese had designs on that school, that's for sure. Anyway, so I went back to the armory, and I was stationed at the armory then. Now, a few days later, the commander of the Hawaiian Territorial Guard--I say National Guard, but it was the National Guard, actually, was called into service, you see. Many of the boys who were eligible for it, the seniors, for example--no, not even the seniors; I guess not. But anyway, the National Guard took in quite a number of acceptable boys, including a lot of the Hawaii Territorial Guard boys, were going to be in the National Guard.
DUNHAM: What is acceptable?
LEE: At least they had some--they were not freshmen. Okay, fine. But the freshmen were to be guarding places with their little rifles--their big rifles [laughs]--in these places. In the meantime, in from the mainland came some rather senior officers from Ohio and Wyoming and Michigan, and they took over the Japanese Consulate. Right? When the Japanese men, the Issei, came from Japan and had children here--I'm sure about the boys; I'm not sure about the girls, but all of the boys' names were then registered with the Japanese Consulate as dual citizens. They could be citizens of Japan or America. Because they were born here, of course, they're Americans; but they could then be citizens of Japan. If the parents wanted to send them back to Japan for education, they could do it without penalty. So it became quite important, for the boys especially--I'm not sure about the girls; I'm sure some of the bright girls must've enlisted, too. But anyway, when one of the Japanese destroyed their confidential records in the Japanese Consulate, the day before the war started--which they did--they did not destroy the records of these local kids. In come our senior officers, and lo and behold, "We've got thousands of spies."
So all the kids who were of Nisei extraction, now they're Nisei, see? They were kicked out of the army, broken hearted. You talk to Ted. He was just absolutely furious. But all these kids were then kicked out. The guns were taken away from them, and they were sent back to school or main thing, but they were not allowed to be soldiers of the American army. This created its own set of animosities between--
So anyway, to make a long story even longer, I guess, we now have this situation. A lot of the local guys--and I can think of some of the names, if I see them in print; I forget who they are; but anyway, I'm pretty sure Ted was one of them, and all these other guys--they're back in the University of Hawaii. Yet they're feeling very, very patriotic towards America, because they were, they were American kids, except that it wasn't realized yet. As you know what happened on the West Coast. The thing is, we didn't have that same situation here, fortunately. But still and all, there was a picking up of people that were suspected, anyway, and put into our camps, too. You know that.
DUNHAM: Yeah. Leaders, elders of the community. Basically, the same.
LEE: Or maybe somebody who took a book subscription in Japanese, from Japan or something like that. In other words, they had these records. You see? But all these other kids went back to the university. But they organized what is known as the Varsity Victory Volunteers, the VVV. They were doing very patriotic things. They were making gardens, they were painting, they were making buildings for the service, doing a lot of things for the service. Eventually, of course, it was realized, "Hey, these kids are all right," you see. But this didn't happen all through 1943. In '43, they were simply students. See? But about towards the end of '43, maybe, and into '44--. '42. No, I'm thinking by '43. Let's say by '43, it was realized that they really were patriotic. So they organized the 442nd Infantry Battalion, just to offer--and they came out with an offer saying, we do have some openings for any of you young fellows who think you might want to be in the Army. They were deluged. [laughs] Every kid on the island, practically, tried to sign up.
So they took a lot of them. Of course, they gave them a very strict health thing and that sort of thing. So they took enough to make a battalion, and then shipped them off to, I guess Mississippi. I think they went to [Camp] Shelby, if I'm not mistaken, and took these kids up. The kids really put themselves to it. So much so that it soon became apparent that no matter who they recruited, they were going to get a good kid. That's really why they first formed the battalion. Then eventually, of course, the 100th Infantry, which was--no, what do they call it? They call it a regiment. I think the 442nd was a regiment.
DUNHAM: The 442nd and the 100th, yeah.
LEE: And the 100th Battalion, I think, yeah. That's right.
DUNHAM: Yeah. So did Ted join?
LEE: Oh, yeah.
DUNHAM: Yeah, he was one of the many who joined, yeah.
LEE: Oh, yeah, sure. I think; I'm not sure. I know that a lot of my friends were. I know that some of those kids in Aiea that I had gone to grade school with were killed in that group of four. Yeah, I know there were. I'm trying to think. {Ohira?}? I'm not sure about the Kuroda brothers. I knew them quite well. Of course, {Nagamini?}. One of the {Nagamini?} boys, I think, was killed. They had a {shotun?} out there in Aiea, they call a store. But heck. Anyway, quite a memory.
DUNHAM: Yeah, yeah. Well, it's very interesting. Well, we've got to end the tape here, and then we should--
LEE: Oh, my gosh!
DUNHAM: It's tape four, on July 26, 2014, with Robert Lee. Yeah, we were just talking; we're a little bit into the US being in the war after Pearl Harbor. You were starting to talk about your mom's role at that time, what she was up to.
LEE: Well, my mother, as I say, she returned to her home on the shore of Pearl Harbor there, next to the water pumping station. That pier that extends out into the harbor from that point, at the present time, it's the Admirals Boathouse. There's a beautiful boat in there with the Admiral. But when we were there, it was a single pier, extending out into the harbor. It was then used as a fleet landing for the crews of the ships which were anchored in the stream to the east and the west of Ford Island. Then those fellows would come into the pier there, and quite a number of them became friends with Mother. She really mothered a lot of those guys. They were all my age. I can remember coming home on liberty sometimes, during 1942. A large part of 1942, I was stationed on Oahu. But I'd come home for the evening sometimes. I had trouble finding a place to sleep, because there was easily a half a dozen to a dozen of these sailors sleeping in the house.
DUNHAM: Wow. So was this just informally that she was helping? She didn't have a formal role?
LEE: Absolutely informally. She was not the USO or anything else. But these were fellows from all over the place, who found a chance to have, you might say, a normal life.
DUNHAM: A home-cooked meal?
LEE: A home-cooked meal, if you will. Also there were just a lot of nice guys, a lot of nice fellows. Some of them actually had their wives writing and stuff like that. So no, she was sort of a surrogate mother, if you will, to awful many of those guys. I know this because I used to go home, and I was also in my sailor uniform, and I could some days hardly be identified, with all the--. Because these fellows were there. And they were good friends with my dad. My dad was, of course, quite a good friend of theirs, too, as you can imagine.
So among the other things that happened, as the days progressed in Pearl Harbor, was recovery of the bodies and so forth. A lot of them, of course, were pulled from ships which had been sunk for quite a while, so it was a very distasteful job to actually bring those fellows up. The Navy put a sawmill at the foot of that pier, and it became the morgue for the recovery of the bodies from the various sunken ships. The bodies would be brought in, and since most of those fellows had not been wearing their dog tags when they died, very many of them had to be identified by dental records. So a body would be brought in, in various stages of decomposition, really, and to be identified, the service records would be taken off the sunken ship at the same time the fellow was sunk. Then service records go wherever the sailor does, and they're put into a waterproof safe. They're brought up, so now we have the service records. So at first, they were brought in and put on the deck right there, in the stretcher. Then the fellows would get down on their hands and knees or so forth, and look into the victim's mouth. Very soon it became apparent--or the Navy didn't come up with any kind of gurney or anything--that's one thing; they were very short supplied of just about everything, you see. So Dad went out and got a couple of saw horses, and they would bring the stretcher in, put it across the sawhorses. But it was still a very unsteady thing.
So one day Mother heard a knock on the door. "Yes, hello, boys." "Mr. Lee said that you have a heavy-duty table up here that we might use." "Oh, anything for the boys." So she gave them this table that Father had constructed out of two-by-fours. It was very sturdy. But she didn't know what it was for; the boys wanted it, and that was it. They took it down and they put it on the pier. Then from then on, any of the gathered bodies were brought on their stretcher and put onto her table.
DUNHAM: This is not the dining room table?
LEE: It was not the dining room table, no. It was a work table that she had in the back of the house. Actually, Dad had made it for her to fold her clothes on, the washing and so on. But it was a very sturdy table. Now, Mother actually, Mother and Dad, were perhaps living fifty yards from where that was all going on, right there. The procedure was to bring a body in, identify it--usually with dental records or what have you--put it into one of the boxes, which were manufactured right there, and then taken up by a flatbed truck to the temporary cemetery, which was up a mile away, in what was called Red Hill. Well, actually, {Halava?}. Well, when you come and go from the other side, you come right through the area where that cemetery was. Now, after a little while, the job is completed, the ships--
DUNHAM: How long a while, about, do you know?
LEE: I'm going to guess recovery of the ships, to bring them up from the harbor, where a lot of them had been sunk, I would say perhaps about six months after the war had started. As much as that.
DUNHAM: Normally, with the association with dead bodies, there's also an incredible stench. Is this true with the water, because they've been waterlogged? Or is that not such an issue then?
LEE: Didn't seem to be an issue.
DUNHAM: Okay, because they'd been in the water, probably.
LEE: I didn't see any--I went out there a few times with them, watched it being done, with my father. Because he was nearby, and he went out quite a bit. The fellows did not wear masks.
DUNHAM: So it must've been because of the water.
LEE: So it couldn't have been bad.
DUNHAM: Did you return to Castle & Cooke, or was that over from that moment?
LEE: No, that was a--it was that long ago. Oh, I remained an employee. Every so often, I'd get a bonus check from them.
DUNHAM: But you didn't go to work for them anymore?
LEE: I didn't go back to work anymore, you see? I went directly from the Hawaiian Territorial Guard straight into the Navy, you see?
DUNHAM: When was that? How long were you in the Hawaiian Territorial Guard?
LEE: I was there until about the end of January, I'd say.
DUNHAM: So how does that evolve over that month and a half, or almost two months, that you're in the Hawaiian Territorial Guard?
LEE: Well, I became the guy in charge of the store. What do they call this?
DUNHAM: The Navy supply store?
LEE: No, no. No, no, nothing like that. It was just the store in the armory. My job then became one of keeping track of the inventory.
DUNHAM: What kind of things were in this store?
LEE: Candy bars.
DUNHAM: Are you the cashier for this store?
LEE: I was the cashier, but I was sort of in charge of making sure that the records stayed straight and so forth. I had an officer over me, too.
DUNHAM: Then is this open to the general public, or who's this store open to?
LEE: No, no, it was just open to the fellows in the Territorial Guard. Eventually, the Territorial Guard was then gathered in from the outside activities like guarding ammunition and so forth. They pulled them all into the armory. We haven't got the time, but I can regale you with a lot of stories about being in the armory. But suffice to say that at one of the times when we were all asleep in the armory--this is after we'd had any number of air raid warnings, where people had had to run out of their houses and jump into these muddy slit trenches and so forth--finally one night, a completely quite night, there were enormous explosions up on Tantalus, the mountain behind the city. It seems that two Japanese patrol planes were detected by the new radar, coming in from the Marianas or someplace, over to Honolulu. They first went over, and we found out later, refueled from submarines at French Frigate Shoals, and then took of and started climbing. By the time they came over Honolulu town they were too high for any of the local airplanes to get up there to shoot at them, or any of the cannon to shoot at them either. So no air raid signs, no nothing; they just let these two patrol planes actually come in and cruise back and forth over Pearl Harbor and so forth. Everything was darked out. They knew they were there, so they shut everything down. So on their way home, these two patrol planes dropped their bombs up on the mountain. Enormous explosions. I forget when it was, but it was almost at that same day that I joined the Navy. I know that I was still in the armory when that happened, though. The whole town shook.
DUNHAM: Was that a coincidence, or was that--?
LEE: No, they just didn't want to fly home with them.
DUNHAM: No, I meant was it a coincidence that you joined the Navy right around that time, or had you been--?
LEE: No, I had applied well before that. But then I was going into the intelligence section, and so the FBI had to clear me, you see?
DUNHAM: Had to vet you, okay. Well, speaking of the FBI, I know we talked a little about, again, the Japanese Americans, friends you'd grown up with, who you knew were fiercely loyal, who were frustrated at not being able to serve. I'm curious also if you knew--maybe not your friends, but more parents or other elders in the community--if you knew of any folks who went away because they were incarcerated here in Hawaii, on Honolulu.
LEE: Yes. Let's see. There was a party--what was their name--Hasabe. I think there was a Hasabe store actually inside Schofield barracks. Everyone knew about that place, and shopped there. He had a going bus there. But he was one that was at least suspected of being one of the storytellers, you might say. You see, the thing is that the Japanese, as you're well aware, I'm sure, spent quite a long time studying Pearl Harbor, and getting a lot of information. When the time came to say, what's happened here, one person in the Japanese Consulate said, "I did it all." So he took everybody off the hook, so it's hard to say that this person was or was not, or that person was or was not.
DUNHAM: Right. Right. But so what was the climate? So martial law is the norm.
LEE: Martial law was very much--well, very much military, of course. But keep in mind that all these folks were part of the population.
DUNHAM: So were there tensions around--again, you've grown up alongside--
LEE: I never detected a single bit of tension. No. And I knew people of quite a number of different ages, too. They did shut down the Japanese schools. There was no such thing as being taught in Japanese. Some of those professors, or some of those teachers, were part of the people that were put into the internment camps.
DUNHAM: What about other Japanese businesses or--?
LEE: There was a Japanese bank here called the--anyway, the Japanese bank was taken over. There were a couple of Japanese stores, or Japanese owned. They were, I want to say, shut down; but I'm not sure, because during the First World War, the German people who were here, their stuff was taken over in World War I, and became such things as the American Factors, the Liberty House. As I say, two or three different businesses that were expropriated, they call it. I know that there were Japanese businesses that were expropriated.
DUNHAM: Right, right. And assets frozen. We mentioned the bank takeover.
LEE: Well, actually, assets taken away from them. In other words, taken.
DUNHAM: Yeah. But you said despite all this, and this concern about spies, you didn't perceive a lot of tension? Because I would think there would have to be some level of tension. What, also I've heard is, because of the tremendous importing of servicemen from the States, too--in the South, in particular--that there also came maybe more tension, because they weren't locals. So that from there, came less sort of openness, I guess you might say.
LEE: Well, I certainly knew a lot of the guys that had come in. I met a lot of them; I worked with quite a few of them. Then of course, my parents knew very many of the guys who were from all these states.
DUNHAM: I'm not trying to put a blanket on all of them, I'm just saying, were there some challenges or friction? Friction is a word you used earlier for an earlier time.
LEE: No, no, I'm just trying to think of any instances where there had been friction. Not in my experience. I can say that, not in my experience. No doubt there were.
DUNHAM: Earlier, you described later, when you went to Michigan, and how you encountered use of he word Jap there, which you voiced an initial clear objection to, and then you had to let it go because they--
LEE: I had to let it go, that's right.
DUNHAM: But you had never heard that in your service time here, or when you went to Maui?
LEE: I never heard it at all, even before the service. Our Japanese friends were "Japanee". I'm a "Japanee." That was acceptable. That's what they called each other anyhow. Or Nippon.
DUNHAM: Yeah. Because I've seen even in the movies, the newsreels from that time--
LEE: Oh, yeah. From that time, that's right.
DUNHAM: --plentiful use of that.
LEE: Da-da-da-da-da-da. Sure.
DUNHAM: Yeah. And lots of caricatures that are--it's wartime.
LEE: Oh, yeah. Well, propaganda.
DUNHAM: Yeah, it's dehumanizing.
LEE: Yeah, it's wartime propaganda, you bet.
DUNHAM: So I would think, to me, in my mind, it's prevalent. It's around a lot, and sort of the norm. But I understand Hawaii's a very unique place, because the Japanese Americans make up such a significant portion of the population.
LEE: Well, look, we were side by side. Gosh, as I say, I had so many friends. Gosh. No, as I say, to me personally, I just never encountered it.
DUNHAM: Yeah. Well, I want to talk about you joining the Navy. But before--I know you said it would be a whole 'nother day of interviews, but--can you give us a little gist of what you meant about when everybody came back into the armory, from the Territorial Guard? What kind of things were going on there? What were you referring to?
LEE: Well, this was a very distressing time for these kids, because they had been, actually, "Hey, we're going to be soldiers." Actually, they had taken on the uniform, they had all the equipment. They were even formed into companies of soldiers. All of a sudden--bang--all this stuff comes down; "No, no, you're out of it." Now, imagine how you would've felt. These guys were--well, most of them went back to university. At that time, we only had--I don't think we had any city colleges to speak of; I think it was all U of H, really. That's where the kids had to go. Very few of them, some few of them went to the mainland to college. But of course, they didn't leave here during the war to go to college. To go to California would've been--
DUNHAM: I was going to say. Yeah, if you were not in the West, it was possible; but I don't know if you could get from here to there without--. But so after the war, you're saying, some of them went to school.
LEE: Well, as the war progressed, folks from the West Coast were able to come and go from Hawaii a little bit, maybe on special trips or something like that. From where I was at, I never had such an opportunity. In other words, I just never thought about going home, once I hit the mainland or anything like that, you see.
DUNHAM: Tell me, is there anything else before you join the Navy. When you're in the armory, you're there full-time? You're not sort of out and about in the community?
LEE: Full-time. Absolutely full time. I'm full time.
DUNHAM: Yeah, so you work at the store a certain amount of hours.
LEE: Work a certain number of hours. That happened to be what I fell into, you might say. But we were still expected to perform our military duties.
DUNHAM: Were you getting any training? Because there is fear of a follow-up attack, invasion. In this Territorial Guard, are you getting any type of training to prepare for that? Or has the military moved in to such an extent that the--?
LEE: Well, the military came in in a big way. There was the case, four days after the war had started, when we in the city started to hear a large airplane roaring sound. Roar. And it got louder and louder, but no call for an air raid or anything like that. But quite a number of us became very disturbed at the sound, because this was right in the period when an expected invasion might occur. So actually, very many of the civilian population became very disturbed at this huge roar of airplanes. What happened was that the Navy put as many aircraft as they could onboard an aircraft carrier and bused it out to Hawaii. But of course, that took three or four days to get here, see. But then all those aircraft took off from the aircraft carrier, when it was still out in the ocean, formed up and came across Honolulu's front door, you see. So once we realized that they were friendly aircraft, everybody was happy. But it was that moment of tension, when you wondered, gee whiz, what is going on? I can remember that. There were several instances like that, where air raids were called, people rushed out of their homes in the middle of the night and jumped into slit trenches, which had been dug all over the place, half full of water, usually.
DUNHAM: Yeah. So are those dug by civilians or the military or both?
LEE: By civilians, mostly.
DUNHAM: So everybody made their own.
LEE: You ask any civilians of the time. They hated the slit trenches. But also people built home bomb shelters, too. Quite a number of those. Oh, yeah. Then let's see. Let me think about life in Hawaii, though.
DUNHAM: Yeah, yeah, civilian life. One of the things I heard, also, because of the fear of invasion--so the thing about some women thinking about, or maybe even cutting their hair or that type of thing. Were there other things people were thinking about? I know it was the curfews and the blackouts, and that's a tremendous--you already described the night of, but that's an ongoing thing. The barbed around? When does the barbed wire go up?
LEE: Well, barbed wire around all the beaches everywhere. The artillery, one by one. Even some of the artillery taken off the sunken ships was then placed in spots over here at Kaneohe, out in the country, at two or three places. Some of the large guns [were] put out there, when they thought an invasion might occur. Yeah. There was also, as I say, as you can imagine, both the banks continued to function. Commerce went on. Sugar cane and pineapples became the source for alcohol. The beer was brought in, in large amounts, from the mainland. Everybody had beer, that's for sure. But there was a rationing here that you have. Gasoline was in short supply. So one of the advantages of having friends in the military, especially the Air Force, was that whenever the Air Force planes came back from a flight, they gasoline had to be drained out, you see. Then it was, of course, thrown away.
DUNHAM: Quote/unquote, "thrown away?"
LEE: Yeah. But believe me, a lot of the automobiles--Model- A Fords ran on 100 percent.
DUNHAM: I had not heard that.
LEE: No, actually, the gasoline was cut with what they call kerosene. Kerosene was available.
DUNHAM: Okay. Yeah. I figured that might be less of an impact here on the island as in some places, the rationing of gasoline, but it still was significant, huh?
LEE: Oh.
DUNHAM: Yeah, it must've been.
LEE: Oh, absolutely. Yeah, that's right.
DUNHAM: Were there other things that were in short supply, relative to rationing, like meat?
LEE: Well, a tire might be recapped three or four times. But the automobiles kept on functioning, that's for sure. Parts were in hard supply, but things were done.
DUNHAM: Yeah. So when you weren't working in the armory, what were you doing? Did you have free time?
LEE: Yeah, I had free time, on what was called liberty. This was when I was still in the armory. I could've taken advantage of Hotel Street, but I didn't.
DUNHAM: Which means what, to take advantage of Hotel Street?
LEE: Hotel Street was the big supply of houses of prostitution. Hotel Street, from one end to the other, was lined with these hotels. But the thing is that the hotels were restricted to a certain area. There's a book by a very well-known author. I know the title of the book; the book is called The Revolt of Mamie Stover [by William Bradford Huie]. The author was a very well-known modern author by the name of--I'll think of it in a minute.
DUNHAM: We can come back to it. We can look it up, too.
LEE: Okay. But anyway, he wrote about one of the so-called madams that they had there. This madam was able to pay off the local politicians, to the point where she decided to spread out. So instead of staying in the simple hotel area of Honolulu, she spread out into some of the valleys, up to Nuuanu Valley, for example. Took over places up there. This was a big, big deal. Eventually, of course, that's what led to the shutting down of all the prostitution here.
DUNHAM: So they had been accepted for some time on Hotel Street, but when they tried to spread--
LEE: Yeah, but you understand, Honolulu was a sailors' town. Down in the area called Iwilei, which is now where all the pineapple canneries are, that always was a low-class area, and a lot of such stuff went on. I'm sorry, but--
DUNHAM: No. Yeah, not at all.
LEE: We had a doctor. In the intelligence, we had our own special doctor. One day he called everybody in. Duty, off-duty, the whole bit, he was going to have a meeting. So we had a meeting in one of the big rooms in the Alexander Young Hotel, where we were based. He said, "Now, listen, you guys." He said, "You're supposed to be of a better class of service than these common, ordinary sailors, so I don't want to catch any of you down there. Because we doctors are assigned. Every week, we go down and we do inspections in these places. Our inspections lead us to believe that they're well taken care of, that they're fairly sterile, and all the people that participate in that are made sure that they're--." What's the term he used? I forget what it is. Prophylactic, is what I think he called it, something like that.
DUNHAM: Yeah, a condom, safe sex.
LEE: He said, "We know all this, and we watch it very carefully. But you people here, I don't want to hear of any of you ever being down there." We took it to heart. He said, "Let's take, for example, that the average prostitute has been flow in from the mainland." Or not flown in, but brought in from various methods or however they got there. But on an average, they would service between forty and fifty men a day, and that the really in-demand ones went up as high as ninety. He said, "This is terrible, but it exists."
