So as I say, we had several different warnings. I was young enough so I didn't care. I didn't get involved with it. But as I say, there were very many young men of my age who did get involved.
DUNHAM: Sure. Well, and there were many, many more men than young women.
LEE: Well, tremendous demand, yeah. Of course, that's one of the reasons why I'm married to a mainland girl. All our girls, all the girls I might've married, growing up with these pretty girls, they disappeared into the arms of Navy and Army officers. We didn't stand a chance. Because a handsome young flier in a uniform? Hard to resist.
DUNHAM: I guess.
LEE: So anyway. Then also they had access to the officers clubs. The of course, do you know about blackout parties?
DUNHAM: I think I've heard that, but tell us.
LEE: Blackout parties occurred. A party could be started, but after curfew--which then got to be as late as eight in the evening--if you were on the streets without a special pass after eight in the evening, you could go to jail. But of course, that meant that if you were a cop, you were not allowed on a military reservation. So all the officers clubs on the military reservations had blackout parties. Everything's overnight. All the young ladies that went there--of course, they were well chaperoned, too, I do know that. They were well chaperoned by officers' wives and stuff like that, so they remained here.
DUNHAM: But you didn't have access to that.
LEE: No, I didn't have access to anything like that, no. That's right. On the other hand, there are many civilian areas that did have blackout parties. But they found ways. They found ways to get to and from where they wanted to go without running into cops. That was a whole technique.
DUNHAM: Did you ever have to utilize techniques?
LEE: Yes, I did.
DUNHAM: What were some of the ways?
LEE: Well, one of the ways here in Wahiawa--now, Wheeler Field, of course, was off limits to the police, right? Wheeler Field. But the Heights, up in there, had a number of housing developments. One that I knew of had an enclosed garage. So that one, they would have a blackout party. But now everybody has to get home. They have to get home. The ladies, of course, would spend the night at one of these homes with the chaperones there. But the GIs, the military officers, who, if picked up by the police, were charged. In other words, they couldn't get away just because they were an officer or anything. So in the one case that I remember, this large double-car garage, a cop was known to be parked down on the corner. This is up in Wahiawa Heights, possibly two miles or more from Wheeler Field gate. So at the time that the G.I.'s had to be back to report in to their duties, they had a military truck up there, one of those military four-by-fours. Not a Jeep, one that held all the guys that were there. It was a large 4x4 truck. Everybody got onto the truck and they flung open the doors of this garage, and the truck raced out, with all this load of army flier officers--they were all officers--and went flying down the road, past the cop, with--darkened--no lights or anything like that. Went tearing down, raced down, and got into the gate at Wheeler Field before the cop was able to catch them.
DUNHAM: The cop is chasing them.
LEE: With the cops chasing them. Yeah, that's right, thanks.
DUNHAM: Can the cop put lights on?
LEE: They had blue lights, at that time.
DUNHAM: What is the relationship between the police and the military?
LEE: Well, they had MPs, and shore patrol. MPs and shore patrol, working out of the Honolulu Police Department, oh, yes.
DUNHAM: So they are working together.
LEE: Oh, yeah, sure. They were working together, that's right. Yeah, that's right. Of course, if the MPs or the shore patrol could have nailed one of their officers, they would've been happy to do it. There was that competition, that's for sure.
DUNHAM: Tell me about joining the Navy. So you joined intelligence?
LEE: Well, I joined the Navy. I joined the Navy directly from Hawaii. Went down to the old naval station. The old naval station was located exactly where Restaurant Row is in Honolulu. You know where Restaurant Row is? Right there. Opposite the pier there was the old naval station, which had been established early in 1900. Before Pearl Harbor, we had a naval station. There were still a number of officers there, and I went down there and swore into the service. Then because I was familiar with Hawaii--we had very many fellows in the intelligence branch, but not many of us from Hawaii. We were taken in from Hawaii. Some few were there, but not very many. In my particular case, I had a driver's license. My father had seen to it that we were pretty familiar with everything on this island, and quite a bit on the other islands, too. My grandfather and so forth, they'd take us there.
So they made me a driver, an automobile driver, you see? So I drove. One of my duties was to drive my captain around, or the other important officers, who said, we've got to get to Kaneohe {Nas?}. How do we get to Kaneohe {Nas?}? Well, one of two or three ways. Okay, well, let's try one of these ways. So I would drive them out and go around Makapu'u. Once I went around Makapu'u, and while I was driving past, the Army Air Force was diving on Rabbit Island. You know where Rabbit Island is? Right off of Makapu'u Point. You know where Life Park is? It looks like a swimming rabbit. Anyway, it's Rabbit Island. They were using it for bombing practice. And to Hawaiians, a real no-no. Of course, the Island of Kaho'olawe was an ocean target, and was constantly bombed and shot at, or heavy artillery was there, because it was uninhabited, supposedly. A lot of goats died, I think. But anyway, that was in the war. But I do remember driving past of Makapu'u Point, just at the time when the army had decided they were going to have dive bombing practice on Rabbit Island, right offshore. So my officer says, "Hey, stop, stop, we want to watch this." So we stopped and watched, while the Army Air Force, Air Corps, bombed Rabbit Island. But they were not explosives. They were just dummy bombs, full of sand or what have you. They would strafe the island and then fly off into the ocean. I'd be willing to bet there's still some of those down in there.
DUNHAM: Were you happy with your assignment?
LEE: Very much so. Very much so. Oh, I was very happy here. But then of course, after the battle at Midway, things loosened up. The Japanese then, of course, had to withdraw from a lot that they had been doing previously. Hawaii had become what was called the Hawaiian Sea Frontier, at that time, which meant that anything beyond Hawaii, right after December 7, was considered dangerous for American ships. One of our Matson ships was sailing. The ship was called the Lahaina. The captain was a good friend of mine. That was {Ernie Roban?}. He was a German citizen, but he was a captain for Matson Navigation Company. Of all things, {Ernie Roban}, as the war started, his ship is on its way to Guam, with a large load of supplies, and it's torpedoed by the Japanese submarines. So {Ernie Roban}, A German skipper, has now been torpedoed. They get into open boats, and they sailed in the open boats, back to Hawaii. It took them about two weeks. But {Ernie} was no dummy, so he had provisioned his lifeboats. So he saved everybody.
DUNHAM: So they all survived?
LEE: Yeah, they got everybody. The ship sank, but he got everybody back to Hawaii. He sailed into--where'd he come in from? Anyway. Kauai, I think, something like that. But I always thought that was ironic, because if he'd been in Germany, he would've been a skipper.
DUNHAM: Interesting. Well, you mentioned earlier--I'm not sure if you were back in Michigan at the time--but you referred to West Loch. Did you know about the West Loch disaster at the time?
LEE: No, I didn't.
DUNHAM: You only know that since?
LEE: The West Loch was completely secret. And Hawaii, of course, had complete censorship. There was a whole division. In fact, many of the local boys actually were, rather than put into intelligence, they were put into censorship.
DUNHAM: Within the Navy or--?
LEE: Navy. All of them were in the Navy, in another place in downtown Honolulu. But a lot of our boys were put in there because--well, they knew a lot of the local jargon, for example. A letter would be going to the mainland, this palikero or that {mohapay?} or what have you. So they could pick that out right away, that sort of thing. So a lot of our local boys were put into censorship. Thank God I didn't get into that; I would've gone crazy.
DUNHAM: Yeah. Why would that have made you crazy?
LEE: Yeah. Well, they had good liberty.
DUNHAM: But what would've been frustrating or crazy-making about that work?
LEE: Yeah.
DUNHAM: Why would you have not wanted to do it?
LEE: Well, as I say, I had a much more interesting job driving around. I had a lot of experiences that I don't think those other guys would've had. These sailors that would come into--that my mother knew so well. Soon after the battle at Coral Sea, before the battle at Midway. I went home to visit Mother, and Mother said, "Gee, it sure is terrible about the Lexington." I said, "Well, the Lexington is one of our aircraft carriers. What happened?" "You didn't know that it was sunk in the Coral Sea battle? All these sailors are starting to be brought back, and they're being kept over on Maui. But the sailors that they talked to them or brought them back are talking about how the battle in the Coral Sea happened. We took a terrible defeat there." I knew nothing about it. It was completely censored. Nothing done about it, nothing said, nothing published.
So the next day I'm driving one of my senior officers out to Pearl Harbor. An intelligence officer. So I'm driving along and I said, "Commander, it's sure a shame about the Lexington." There's this silence. He says, "What do you know about the Lexington?" I said, "Well, I know it's been sunk in the Coral Sea." "Where did you hear that?" He's just up in the air. I said, "Well, it's all over Pearl Harbor, don't you know? You must know it." He said, "Yeah, but," he said, "you're not supposed to know." I said, "Tell that to all the sailors." Oh, that was funny.
DUNHAM: Yeah. So with West Loch, do you know if it was being talked about?
LEE: I knew there was something that happened, because Mother wrote me. In one of her letters, she said, "Something really big has happened here in Pearl Harbor." She didn't say it was terrible or it was this, that, and the other thing. But every one of her letters had--chhh--little black marks. No, I think they cut them in those days.
DUNHAM: Oh, they cut them out?
LEE: They cut them out, yeah.
DUNHAM: Cut out portions of letters.
LEE: Yeah, they did.
DUNHAM: Do you have any of those letters?
LEE: No.
DUNHAM: I would like to see. It's interesting how it was actually censored.
LEE: I'm just wondering. I've got some old letters in there. Maybe if I find one, I'll send it to you.
DUNHAM: I'd be interested to see. So she wrote to and referenced West Loch, but you think that was one that was censored, whatever she tried to convey.
LEE: Yeah. That's right, yeah. Yeah, I had to write back and tell her I was thinking about--I had a wonderful girlfriend, much better than the ones back there.
DUNHAM: So that didn't get censored.
LEE: That didn't get censored. Anything going in that direction, I think, was--
DUNHAM: In the years since, have you learned about West Loch then, or is it still--?
LEE: Oh, yeah. As soon as I came home. In fact, it was even before I came home. One of the sailors who, for some reason, came to the university was talking about it. He said, "You're from Hawaii." I said, "Yeah, yeah, I'm from Hawaii. What's going on in Pearl?" Then he told me about it. I guess a very large percent of the--well, there were, I think, seven or eight of the LSTs actually destroyed there. If you were to go to West Loch today, you'll still see--one of the LST's bows is sticking out of the water. Most of them were picked up and shipped out. That's when they think that they got rid of the two unknown--
DUNHAM: The missing submarines?
LEE: Japanese submarines, yeah. They were called midgets, really, two-man submarines.
DUNHAM: But some kind of accident, presumably, that took place there at West Loch in '44 or--. No?
LEE: Well, actually, no, it was just a big thing that had happened at Pearl Harbor. So I don't know what it was, and so forth.
DUNHAM: So you think it's still a mystery, what actually happened.
LEE: Yeah, yeah. I don't think anybody really knows, because a lot of that stuff is still on the bottom on the ocean, but it's a couple of miles down. They've got other things to do. You know, of course, that there are three Japanese submarines out there, too, right?
DUNHAM: Well, that's what I'd heard.
LEE: Three very large Japanese submarines. The Japanese submarines were twice the size of ours. Had compartments for, for example, wing clipped airplanes, stuff like that. At the time of the battle at Midway, you see, because those two aircraft who had come over to bomb Hawaii in January or February--I think it was January, end of January--because they had refueled at French Frigate Shoals, and the Nimitz then put two or three of the destroyers on station out there, so the Japanese then sent their submarines back to detect Hawaii. One of the things that they did very quickly, as you know, the battle [of] the Midway was where they took a year to study Pearl Harbor, and it took them about a week and a half to study Midway. Of course, you know the whys of Midway. The Doolittle Raid did a lot to create Midway, as you know.
Anyway, it was only after that, in other words, that the Japanese were prevented from, you might say, setting up a picket line of submarines between Hawaii and Midway. Even if they had, by the time they got there, our carriers were out beyond that area. But anyway, they could not surface, and the patrol planes that they anticipated would be refueled there couldn't be done. So we know that. A lot of us knew that part of it, or we found out of it soon after Midway. But anyway, the battle of Midway, it made a big change here, because from that time on, we started bringing in WAVES. We had a large contingent of Wahine, of women that could do just the same job we were doing. So myself and a lot of others were then sent out to general service.
DUNHAM: When was that, that you left for general service?
LEE: I left, I'd say, towards the end of '42. By '43, early, I was already on Maui. The navy had spent 1942 in constructing the air field at Maui. But that air field was used to train carrier pilots, even before we had the carriers. The pilots were trained in low-powered aircraft, and then brought out to Hawaii in many numbers, and trained on, you might say, carrier aircraft. Much higher power. One of the things that I participated in--I was still a yeoman. When I went into service I was very fortunate, in that I was put into service in Maui, under the command of a very great lieutenant commander, who was the communications officer. He was just wonderful. He was just a real king. He was a Chicago lawyer, is what he was. He sure knew his work. Anyway, that goes on and on, but I thought he was a tremendously real nice man. He's the one I went to Michigan for. Finally my orders came through. I had applied for OCS, to go to OCS, but the navy said no, they had other uses [for me]. But towards the end of 1943, my orders came through that I was to appear in Michigan for the 1944 February semester, starting in a university someplace in the mainland. So I said, "Oh, well, I'd like to be away from the West Coast." I'd been there. So I thought maybe I'd go to, oh, Kansas or Wisconsin or something like that. He said, "Michigan." That's where he had been. So I went to Michigan.
DUNHAM: What were you studying there?
LEE: Well we started, the first year, we were going to be in engineering; but I was not in engineering, so they very quickly switched me to supply corps. I was going to be a supply officer. I was doing pretty well. But I got into Michigan well in advance of the start of the semester, at least a full week ahead.
DUNHAM: Oh, so you had a little time.
LEE: Because I had no reason to--I had family on the West Coast. My aunt and her retired husband lived there, as well as my older sister may have been there. No, she wasn't there yet. But anyway, this was the only relative, so I visited them for a while, visited my grandmother's parents down in Southern California, and then I thought, "What the heck? I've got nothing else to do." I had no particular reasons, so I then got on a train and took five days--five days of train, a very open train. It was so old--it was a steam train--it was so old that the heating--this was winter--the heating for the train car was a coal stove in the front of the car. [chuckles] That's right. But of course, it was a civilian train. I can't imagine how many sidings we were on, because a troop train would be coming through, so we'd be put on a siding. Then a troop train or a supply train or something would go rushing through. So they're on their way; but meanwhile, this train that I'm on--same car, same train, same steam engine, same everything; different crews, of course, because they come out from divisions.
But it took us a full five days, in a chair car, from San Francisco down to Los Angeles; and in Los Angeles--how was that? I started in San Francisco, then went down to Los Angeles. If you've ever been in Los Angeles, they have a beautiful, beautiful railroad station down there. I remember that so well. Then from there, then I went down through the southern route, El Paso and Albuquerque and all those little Western towns. Finally wound up coming into Chicago, by way of St. Louis and Kansas City, I think. But when I got to Chicago, I called up the parents of one of the fellows that I was serving with in Hawaii. She said, "We've been expecting you." Because I'd told my friend, "Yeah, I'm going to go. I'll stop in Chicago and say hello to your folks." "We've been expecting you." So she told me how to get a taxi there. She said, "Get a taxi and come to this address." They had a very nice apartment on the shores at Lake Michigan. Very beautiful place, several rooms in the apartment; it was whole apartment. I got to the door and knocked on the door, and the door opened up, and here's this nice lady there. She says, "Follow me. Hello Bob, follow me." She took me in, and she opened up another door, and they already had a hot tub waiting for me and clean underwear and the whole thing. I thought, "What a thing!"
DUNHAM: After your five days on the train, that was the perfect thing, huh?
LEE: Five days on the train. Had shaving gear and the whole bit. So I was a new man, I'll tell you, when I finished there.
DUNHAM: I was wondering, you mentioned about your mom here telling you about West Loch. Did you ever hear of the Port Chicago explosion in July of '44?
LEE: Yes. Yes, we know that. But that was earlier.
DUNHAM: Well, no, it should've been July 17 of '44, a couple months after West Loch, is what I have. Because they both just had their seventieth anniversaries.
LEE: Yeah, but they didn't keep that same kind of secret.
DUNHAM: No, right, they didn't have the same secrecy, so it came out earlier, probably.
LEE: Oh, yeah, that's right, yeah.
DUNHAM: But do you remember hearing of it?
LEE: Yeah, absolutely. Absolutely.
DUNHAM: What did you hear of it at the time?
LEE: That there had been an explosion so great that it had practically destroyed the town. But then of course, everybody said, "Well, that's just like the one in the East, in World War I, where--Gander, or that area in Canada."
DUNHAM: I don't think I know that.
LEE: You didn't know about that one?
DUNHAM: No, no, I should know.
LEE: Oh. Oh, absolutely. World War I.
DUNHAM: There was a munitions loading-type explosion.
LEE: Munitions loading, yeah. It destroyed not Gander, but that same area.
DUNHAM: That's about where it was?
LEE: What's the big city up there?
DUNHAM: I don't know what state that is, even, sorry.
LEE: Well, it's in Canada. Yeah. It was in Newfoundland, I think.
DUNHAM: No, I don't know about that. So World War I, an explosion.
LEE: Yeah. Oh, it leveled the town.
DUNHAM: Very interesting. Well, we're almost near the end of our time, I think. There's a lot of things I'd like to ask you, but I know you volunteer three days a week and enjoy that very much. I wanted to ask you, I guess, about looking back, reflecting, and what that means to do that work, or any closing thoughts you have, anything we haven't asked you about today that you'd like to speak to, before we close. I haven't asked you about the medal that you're wearing, as well.
LEE: Oh. This was given to me by the Pearl Harbor memorial. They give this to World War II veterans, you see. That's what I am, a World War II veteran. I don't have a distinguished military career, by any means, because I was just--well, as I say, first of all, I was in, you might say, the Territorial Guard, watching a store.
DUNHAM: Well, but you were there right away.
LEE: But then I joined the Navy, and the Navy had me basically driving Navy officers around, really. Then I moved over to Maui. At that point, that's probably the most important part of my Navy career, in that I participated, with my communications officer, in keeping things going. As a yeoman, he took me one or two places. We flew into Pearl once or twice, while he was doing work, and he brought me along. But I was only a third class yeoman, by then. In other words, I had a long way to go, so I didn't take dictation or anything. But he was a nice guy. That's one of the reasons I liked him so much, because when he went someplace he would take me.
DUNHAM: Well, that's a nice thing for a boss to do.
LEE: So I got to fly in and out of Pearl, come to see the folks and so forth. Then when I finally went into what was called V-12 in the OCS school, taking fellows either from college or from already in service--which I was--and putting them in college, you see. When I went to university at Michigan, I was there several days early. But the very first day that I got there, I got off the train in the middle of the night, and got a taxi and went up to where the sailors were supposedly living. Everyone was gone; they were all on semester break. I walked into this place, and there were a couple of Navy chiefs there. It was a Navy chief and an old retired Navy chief, and a couple other guys. "What are you doing here?" I said, "Well, I'm here. I'm on duty here." So they put me up in one of the barracks room. It's the west quad; it was student housing. Beautiful place, really. But when I woke up in the morning, the lady who was in charge of the housekeeping, she said, "Oh, my goodness." Nice little old German lady. "What are you doing here?" I said, "Well, I had nothing else to do, and I didn't have money for a hotel or anything like that, and I didn't want to go anywhere else, so I came to the university." She said, "Oh, well, that's nice." So we talked back and forth. Turned out to be my [future] mother-in-law.
DUNHAM: Oh, wow.
LEE: Because what happened was--she was a very nice lady. Really was, she was a peach. So we talked back and forth and became acquainted. A few days later, we talked again. She said, "Oh, you're here by yourself. One of these days, come to my house and we'll have a nice dinner or something." I said, "Well, no, I can't do that." So anyway, we continued conversing and being friends. Then one day she said, "You sure you don't want to come over for dinner or something like that?" I said, "Well, sure, I will." She said, "Good. Myself and my daughter, we'd be glad to entertain you." I didn't know, but she was vetting me. Because I was really a pretty simple guy. I was not a foul-mouthed sailor or anything like that, and I got along pretty well. But she was a nice lady, too. Anyway, so I went over there. I went over to her house, which was nearby, and knocked on the door. She had an apartment there. Just a small, simple apartment. She said, "Well, come on in. I have to tell you that Daughter has decided she has another thing to do, so she won't be in," see, like that. Daughter told me later that she had told her mother, "You're not going to get me a date!" See, or anything like that. But she did. She was visiting with a friend of hers. Looked out the window; I didn't look too bad.
DUNHAM: She was screening you, huh?
LEE: So then she decided to go home. So she did come home afterwards--
DUNHAM: That's funny.
LEE: --and then we met and conversed and became friends.
DUNHAM: And the rest is history there, huh?
LEE: Isn't that nice? But I thought that was funny. That's a funny story to me, because--
DUNHAM: Oh, yeah, that's very sweet.
LEE: --I knew her mother a good deal.
DUNHAM: And that ended up being your wife. Yeah. Well, I don't want to skip seventy years, but we just have about one minute left, and I just thought Pearl Harbor, obviously such a central place for you and your family, your mom and dad. Just could you briefly sum up, I guess, what it means today to be a volunteer there and to continue to share these stories, and that you've shared with us today?
LEE: Well, my stories there are all about Pearl Harbor. I do tell them that I was living there, on the shores with my parents. I list myself as witness to the attack. They call me a survivor there. And I did participate in a lot that went on, because during the day, until I left in the afternoon to go with the ambulance, I participated in a lot of things. I washed a lot of fellows down, shot at Japanese planes with my 22.
DUNHAM: Yeah. You did a lot of things within twenty-four hours. Your family, too, your mom and dad.
LEE: Then, also that was a boat pool then. The pier there was a boat pool. So one of the things that we did was more or less act as a sort of a guide, really. People came to the pier. But I stayed pretty much on the pier; I did not go out in the harbor. Other people went out into the harbor. Some had the experience--the boy who married my cousin, eventually, was on one of those boats. I had a cousin who married him. He went out and he started pulling these people out of the water, you see. As I say, I didn't get into that, I have to say. But I did do a lot of things, and I'm just trying to think.
DUNHAM: Well, you did a lot of things. Our tape's going to run out. Yeah, I just want to thank you for sharing all that you've shared today, and for you continuing to share your experiences with all the visitors that you encounter there at the center.
LEE: Well, I talked about that. As I say, I talk about the things that I saw--the aircraft coming over, and being shot at. When those Japanese were circling around, we didn't have to look up at them.
